title_zh: 正精進的感恩 · The Laughing Buddha 歡喜佛

pills.label: Principle
pills.text: Right Effort · Sammā Vāyāma 正精進

objectives.html: Use the patterns “I feel…” and “I am grateful for…” to express feelings, and learn positive vocabulary: grateful, joyful, effort, diligent, present. Use the simple present tense for daily habits.
objectives.zh_html: 運用句型「I feel…（我覺得…）」與「I am grateful for…（我感謝…）」來表達感受，並學習正向單字：grateful（感恩）、joyful（喜悅）、effort（努力）、diligent（勤奮）、present（當下）。用簡單現在式描述每天的習慣。

objectives.html: Understand Right Effort (Sammā Vāyāma) as the steady cultivation of wholesome states like gratitude and joy — and connect “letting go” of pressure (the Laughing Buddha) with awareness of the present moment.
objectives.zh_html: 認識「正精進（Sammā Vāyāma）」是持續培養感恩、喜悅等善心的功夫；並把「放下」壓力（歡喜佛的智慧）與覺察當下連結起來。

examples.html: I feel happy.
examples.say: I feel happy.

examples.html: I feel joyful and calm.
examples.say: I feel joyful and calm.

examples.html: I feel present when I breathe slowly.
examples.say: I feel present when I breathe slowly.
examples.zh: 當我慢慢呼吸時，我安住於當下。

examples.html: I am grateful for my teacher.
examples.say: I am grateful for my teacher.

examples.html: I am grateful for my family, because they are kind.
examples.say: I am grateful for my family, because they are kind.
examples.zh: 我感謝我的家人，因為他們很善良。

examples.html: I study English every day.
examples.say: I study English every day.

examples.html: Consistency requires effort.
examples.say: Consistency requires effort.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Simple present for habits
grammar.html: We use the simple present tense to talk about things we do again and again. Add -s for he / she / it: She studies every day. · He works hard. Right Effort means doing a little, consistently — not everything in one burst.
grammar.zh_html: 我們用「簡單現在式」來談一再重複做的事。主詞是 he／she／it 時，動詞要加 -s：She studies every day.（她每天讀書。）· He works hard.（他很努力。）正精進的意思是：每天做一點、持續不斷——而不是一口氣全做完。

paras: Long ago in China there lived a wandering monk named Budai （布袋和尚）. He was round and cheerful, and he carried a big cloth bag everywhere he went. People called him the Laughing Buddha because he was always smiling.

Inside his bag he kept small gifts for children, and he gave them away wherever he traveled. He owned almost nothing, yet he was the happiest person in every village. When people asked him the secret of happiness, he simply put down his bag and laughed.

The bag was heavy — like the worries and pressure we carry in our minds. Budai showed that we can work hard and walk far, and still set the bag down and rest in the present moment with a joyful heart. That is the balance of Right Effort: try your best, and let go of the stress.

items: Why did Budai carry a big bag? 他為什麼背著大袋子？
Why do people call him the Laughing Buddha? 為什麼叫他「歡喜佛」？
What does “putting down the bag” mean for you? 「放下袋子」對你來說是什麼意思？

story.figcaption: This week’s visual: How to feel happy every day — effort, gratitude, and the present moment.
paras_zh: 很久以前，中國有一位雲遊的出家人，名叫布袋和尚。他身形圓胖、笑容滿面，走到哪兒都背著一個大布袋。因為他總是笑瞇瞇的，大家就叫他「歡喜佛」。
布袋裡裝著要送給孩子們的小禮物，他走到哪兒就分送到哪兒。他幾乎一無所有，卻是每個村莊裡最快樂的人。當人們問他快樂的祕訣，他只是把布袋放下，呵呵大笑。
那個布袋很重——就像我們心裡背負的種種煩惱與壓力。布袋和尚告訴我們：可以努力工作、走得很遠，同時也能把袋子放下，帶著歡喜的心安住當下。這就是「正精進」的平衡：盡力而為，也要放下壓力。

principle.lede: Watch this short film, The Ru-Yi Method, then think about how effort and joy work together.
principle.h4: Effort, the Middle Way 中道的精進
principle.html: Right Effort is not pushing yourself until you break, and it is not being lazy. It is the steady, kind effort of doing a little every day — and meeting each moment with gratitude. When you study English with joy instead of fear, the words come more easily. Diligence and laughter belong together.
principle.lede_zh: 觀看這部短片《如意之道》(The Ru-Yi Method)，然後想一想：努力與喜悅如何相輔相成。
principle.zh_html: 正精進不是把自己逼到崩潰，也不是懈怠偷懶。它是一種溫和而持續的努力——每天做一點，並以感恩的心面對每個當下。當你帶著「喜悅」而非「恐懼」學英文時，字句會更容易記住。勤奮與歡笑，本來就該在一起。

practice.note: Write in your journal every day this week. Choose the tier that fits you — the daily practice is the Right Effort. 每天寫一篇，選一個適合自己的等級。

tiers.req: Write 1 sentence using “I feel…” or “I am grateful for…” plus a vocabulary word. Name one happy feeling.
tiers.ex: ExampleI am grateful for my mom. I feel joy.
tiers.req_zh: 用「I feel…」或「I am grateful for…」加上一個本週單字，寫一個句子，說出一種快樂的感受。

tiers.req: Write 3–4 sentences. Use a simple-present verb and a reason with because. Connect the gratitude to a feeling and a person.
tiers.ex: ExampleI am grateful for my friend, because she is very kind. We played a game today. I feel joyful.
tiers.req_zh: 寫三到四個句子。用一個簡單現在式動詞，並用 because（因為）說明理由，把感恩連結到一種感受和一個人。

tiers.req: Write 5+ sentences using a complex structure (although, when, since) and reflect on the Right Effort principle.
tiers.ex: ExampleAlthough studying is hard, I am grateful for the effort I am making. I feel present and peaceful when I laugh.
tiers.req_zh: 寫五句以上，運用複雜句型（although 雖然、when 當、since 既然），並反思「正精進」的道理。

quiz.text: Which word means “感恩的 / thankful”?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Grateful means thankful (感恩的). Diligent = hard-working; present = here, now.

quiz.text: Complete the feeling: “I ___ joyful today.”
quiz.expl: Correct: C. We say “I feel + (feeling word).” → I feel joyful.

quiz.text: Choose the correct sentence to give thanks.
options: I am grateful for my teacher.
quiz.expl: Correct: D. The pattern is “I am grateful for ___.” Don’t forget the word for.

quiz.text: Habits use the simple present. Which is correct?
options: She to study English every day.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. For he / she / it, add -s (and study → studies).

quiz.text: In English, the Laughing Buddha’s big bag stands for our…
quiz.expl: Correct: C. The heavy bag is a picture of the worries and pressure we carry. “Putting it down” means letting go of stress.

quiz.text: “Right Effort” (Sammā Vāyāma) means…
options: waiting until you feel like it
steady, joyful effort a little every day
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Right Effort is the Middle Way — consistent, kind effort, not one big exhausting burst.

quiz.text: Which word means “當下 / here and now”?
quiz.expl: Correct: A. The present moment = 當下. Enlightenment = 覺悟; laughter = 笑聲.

quiz.text: A diligent student is one who…
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Diligent (勤奮的) describes someone who works hard and steadily — the heart of Right Effort.

quiz.text: Add a reason: “I am grateful for my friend ___ she is kind.”
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Because introduces a reason: “…grateful for my friend because she is kind.”

quiz.text: What lesson does the Laughing Buddha teach about studying?
options: Hard work is a waste of time.
You should never make any effort.
Work hard, and also let go of stress with joy.
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Diligence and laughter belong together — try your best and set down the heavy bag of pressure.

title_zh: 正念與當下 · Sati 念 · 平靜心猿意馬

objectives.html: Use the Present Continuous tense (“I am + V-ing”) to describe what is happening right now, and learn key vocabulary for calm and mindfulness: stress, calm, deep breathing, peace, mindful, technique.

Understand Mindfulness (Sati) as paying attention to the present moment without judgment. Practice the Mind-Body-Heart connection and deep breathing to settle the restless “Monkey Brain” and ease stress (Dukkha).

patterns.label: Pattern 1 · What I am doing right now

examples.html: I am breathing slowly.
examples.say: I am breathing slowly.

examples.html: I am sitting quietly.
examples.say: I am sitting quietly.

examples.html: I am feeling calm now.
examples.say: I am feeling calm now.

patterns.label: Pattern 2 · He / She / It is doing

examples.html: The monkey is jumping.
examples.say: The monkey is jumping.

examples.html: My mind is racing today.
examples.say: My mind is racing today.

patterns.label: Pattern 3 · Asking and answering about now
patterns.formula: What are you doing? — I am ___ing.

examples.html: What are you doing when you feel calm?
examples.say: What are you doing when you feel calm?

examples.html: I am drawing, and I am taking deep breaths.
examples.say: I am drawing, and I am taking deep breaths.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Present Continuous (現在進行式)
grammar.html: We use the Present Continuous to talk about what is happening right now — the present moment. The structure is Subject + am / is / are + V-ing: I am breathing. · She is sitting. · The monkey is jumping. The V-ing form means “正在做…”. Use am with I, is with he / she / it, and are with you / we / they.

paras: Imagine a little monkey living inside your head （心猿意馬）. It never sits still. It jumps from branch to branch — one moment thinking about lunch, the next about a test, the next about a game. This restless, jumping mind is what we call the “Monkey Brain.”

When the monkey jumps too much, we feel stress. Our chest gets tight and our thoughts race. But we do not need to fight the monkey or chase it. We only need to do one quiet thing: notice it. “Oh — my mind is jumping right now.” That noticing, without judging, is Mindfulness (Sati).

Then we take a slow deep breath. We place one hand on our mind, one on our body, one on our heart, and we feel them come together in the present moment. Little by little, the monkey grows quiet, sits down, and rests. We are calm — not because nothing is happening, but because we are fully here, right now.

items: What is the “Monkey Brain”? 什麼是「心猿意馬」？
What is the first step to calm the monkey? 讓心猿安靜的第一步是什麼？
When does your Monkey Brain jump the most? 你的心什麼時候最躁動？

practice.note: Each day this week, journal about one moment of stress and one technique you used to feel calm — using the Present Continuous tense. Choose the tier that fits you. 每天寫一篇，用現在進行式記錄一次壓力與一個平靜的方法。

tiers.req: Write 2 sentences. Use a V-ing form once, and use the word calm or peace. Then draw a picture of your “calm feeling” (or a settled monkey).
tiers.ex: ExampleI feel calm now. I am breathing deeply.

tiers.req: Write 4–5 sentences using at least 3 new words (stress, deep breathing, mindful). Describe one stressor and one calm action with V-ing.
tiers.ex: ExampleToday, I was feeling stress because I was doing my math homework. Now, I am sitting quietly. I am mindful of my body.

tiers.req: Write 5+ sentences with a complex structure (when, while, although) and a personal 3-step calmness strategy. Explain your “Monkey Brain.”
tiers.ex: ExampleMy Monkey Brain is jumping a lot when I am preparing for tests. First, I am taking deep breaths. Secondly, I am stopping my thoughts. Lastly, I am smiling to release the pressure.

quiz.text: Which word means “平靜的 / not stressed”?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Calm means peaceful and relaxed (平靜的). Stress = 壓力; technique = 方法.

quiz.text: The Present Continuous describes actions happening…
options: every single day as a habit
quiz.expl: Correct: B. “I am + V-ing” describes what is happening right now — the present moment.

quiz.text: Choose the correct Present Continuous sentence.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. The pattern is am / is / are + V-ing: “I am breathing slowly.”

quiz.text: Fill the blank: “The monkey ___ jumping.”
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Use is with he / she / it (a monkey = it): “The monkey is jumping.”

quiz.text: In English, the “Monkey Brain” (心猿意馬) means our…
quiz.expl: Correct: C. The “Monkey Brain” is a picture of the uncontrolled, restless mind that jumps from thought to thought.

quiz.text: What is the first step of Mindfulness (Sati) when the monkey jumps?
options: Shout at your mind to stop
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Mindfulness means simply noticing what the mind is doing right now, without judgment — then returning to the breath.

quiz.text: Which is a healthy technique to calm down?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Deep breathing (深呼吸) is a wholesome technique that replaces restless stress with calm — it is Right Effort.

quiz.text: Mindfulness (Sati) means paying attention to the present moment…
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Sati is paying gentle attention to the here-and-now without judgment — not punishing yourself for being distracted.

quiz.text: Answer the question: “What are you doing now?” →
quiz.expl: Correct: C. To describe what is happening now, use the Present Continuous: “I am sitting quietly.”

quiz.text: Why do we calm the Monkey Brain with the breath?
options: So we can win arguments with friends
To return to the present moment and ease stress
To make our minds completely empty forever
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Coming back to the breath brings us into the present moment, softening stress (Dukkha) and restlessness with peace.

title_zh: 業與果報 · Karma / Cetanā 意圖的力量

pills.label: Principle
pills.text: Karma & Intention · Cetanā 業與意圖

objectives.html: Use the simple past tense to talk about yesterday, and master three key irregular verbs: sleep → slept, eat → ate, go → went. Learn the words intentional, result, cause.
Understand karma / Cetanā — that every action begins with an intention. Learn to look back on the past with wisdom, not worry, so that we can understand our intentions and grow from them.

patterns.label: Pattern 1 · Saying what you did (irregular verbs)

examples.html: I slept at nine o’clock.
examples.say: I slept at nine o'clock.

examples.html: I ate an apple this morning.
examples.say: I ate an apple this morning.

examples.html: I went to school with my friend.
examples.say: I went to school with my friend.

examples.html: What did you eat yesterday?
examples.say: What did you eat yesterday?

examples.html: Did you feel calm yesterday?
examples.say: Did you feel calm yesterday?

patterns.label: Pattern 3 · Giving a reason (cause and result)

examples.html: I ate rice because I was hungry.
examples.say: I ate rice because I was hungry.

examples.html: I slept well because I felt peaceful.
examples.say: I slept well because I felt peaceful.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Simple past tense and irregular verbs
grammar.html: The simple past tense describes a finished action — something that already happened and cannot be changed. Many verbs add -ed (help → helped), but our three key verbs are irregular: sleep → slept · eat → ate · go → went. To ask a question, use did + the base verb: Did you go to school? (not Did you went).

paras: Imagine a gardener who plants a seed. （想像一位園丁種下一顆種子。） If she plants a kind seed — a helpful word, a generous act — a kind plant grows. If she plants a careless seed, weeds grow instead. The seed is the cause （原因）; the plant is the result （結果）. We are all gardeners of our own day.

So when we look back at yesterday, we are not looking for things to worry about. We look back with wisdom: “Why did I do that? What was my intention?” Maybe yesterday I forgot my homework, and this caused stress this morning. That is a small result of a small cause — and now I can plant a wiser seed today. Don’t worry about yesterday; learn from it.

items: What does the seed stand for in the story? 故事裡的「種子」代表什麼？
Is karma the same as fate? Why or why not? 業力等於命運嗎？為什麼？
When we look back at yesterday, should we worry — or learn? 回顧昨天時，我們該擔心還是學習？

principle.lede: In Buddhism, karma (業) is not fate or punishment. The Buddha taught: “It is intention (Cetanā 思) that I call karma.” Every action — what we say, do, and think — begins with an intention, and that intention shapes the results we meet today.
principle.h4: Intention is the seed 意圖即種子
principle.html: Cetanā (思) means volition or intention — the small spark inside us right before we act. We cannot change yesterday’s actions, but we can understand the intentions behind them. When we plant wholesome intentions today, we shape a kinder result tomorrow. Looking back becomes a teacher, not a burden.

practice.note: Each day, write down what you did using the simple past tense, and reflect on the intention behind it. Choose the tier that fits you. 每天用過去式記錄，並反思行為的意圖與結果。

tiers.req: Write 3 complete sentences that use all three target verbs: slept, ate, went. Then say one good thing that happened today.
tiers.ex: ExampleI slept at nine o’clock. I ate an apple. I went to school.

tiers.req: Write 4–5 sentences using at least 5 past-tense verbs. Describe one wholesome action and explain why it was helpful.
tiers.ex: ExampleYesterday, I helped my dad. I felt happy because I made a good action. I slept very well after that.

tiers.req: Write 5+ sentences using complex structures (because of, although, after that) and analyze the intention (Cetanā) behind one action.
tiers.ex: ExampleI forgot to finish my homework last night. Although this caused stress this morning, I reflected on my intention and promised to be more mindful of my time today.

quiz.text: What is the simple past tense of sleep ?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Sleep is irregular: sleep → slept. There is no “sleeped.”

quiz.text: Choose the correct sentence about yesterday.
options: I ate an apple this morning.
I eat an apple this morning.
I eated an apple this morning.
I eating an apple this morning.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Eat is irregular: eat → ate. We use the past tense for a finished action.

quiz.text: Complete it: “Yesterday I ___ to school.”
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Go is irregular: go → went. “Goed” is never correct.

quiz.text: Which question is correct?
options: Did you went to the park?
Did you go to the park?
Did you going to the park?
Do you went to the park?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. After did, use the base verb: “Did you go…?” — not “did you went.”

quiz.text: In Buddhism, what does karma (Cetanā) really mean?
options: Bad luck that we cannot change
Intention — the spark behind our actions
quiz.expl: Correct: C. The Buddha said, “It is intention (Cetanā) that I call karma.” Karma begins with our intention, not with fate.

quiz.text: In the garden story, the seed is the cause. What is the plant?
quiz.expl: Correct: A. The seed is the cause (原因) and the plant that grows is the result (結果).

quiz.text: Why do we look back at yesterday?
options: To worry about our mistakes all day
To learn from our intentions with wisdom
quiz.expl: Correct: D. We look back with wisdom, not worry — to understand our intentions and plant a wiser seed today.

quiz.text: Which word means “結果 / what happens after a cause”?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Result = 結果. Cause = 原因; intention = 意圖.

quiz.text: Add a reason: “I slept well ___ I felt peaceful.”
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Because introduces a reason — a cause: “I slept well because I felt peaceful.”

quiz.text: A student forgot her homework and felt stressed. What is the wise response?
options: Reflect on the intention and choose to be more mindful today.
Worry about it for the whole week.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. We cannot change the past, but we can understand the intention behind it and plant a wiser seed today.

title_zh: 發願與慈悲行 · Refuge & Mettā 皈依與慈心

pills.label: Principle
pills.text: Refuge & Loving-Kindness · Mettā 皈依與慈悲

pills.label: English
pills.text: Future tense · “I am going to…” · “I will…”

objectives.html: Use the future tense to express plans and promises: “I am going to…” (a plan) and “I will…” (a decision). Learn kindness vocabulary: commitment, compassion, humility, the Middle Path, stranger, intention.

Understand Refuge (皈依) as setting a goal guided by the Buddha’s teaching, and practice Mettā (慈悲) by turning loving-kindness into a real, generous act — performed with pure intention and no wish for reward.

examples.html: I am going to smile at a stranger.
examples.say: I am going to smile at a stranger.

examples.html: I am going to help someone, because it is kind.
examples.say: I am going to help someone, because it is kind.

examples.html: I am going to act with compassion this week.
examples.say: I am going to act with compassion this week.

examples.html: I will help them.
examples.say: I will help them.

examples.html: I will speak in a kind voice.
examples.say: I will speak in a kind voice.

examples.html: My intention is to show kindness with humility.
examples.say: My intention is to show kindness with humility.

examples.html: My intention is pure, so I expect nothing in return.
examples.say: My intention is pure, so I expect nothing in return.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Future tense — “going to” vs. “will”
grammar.html: Both talk about the future, but with a small difference. Use “am / is / are going to” for a plan you have already decided: I am going to volunteer tomorrow. Use “will” for a promise or decision made in the moment: I will help you. A commitment to the Buddha’s path (Right Thought) is exactly this kind of clear promise.

paras: When the Buddha sat beneath the Bodhi tree, many troubles came to test him. We call these troubles Mara （魔羅，生命中的困難） — the doubts and fears that try to pull us off the path. The Buddha did not fight them with anger, and he did not run away. He met them with a calm, steady heart, walking the Middle Path （中道） — not too tight, not too loose.

After his awakening, the Buddha did not keep his peace for himself. He set an intention: to teach with compassion (Mettā) so that others could find the same freedom. To follow him, students take Refuge （皈依） — they choose his teaching as their guide, like a traveler choosing the North Star.

Refuge is not only words. It becomes real the moment we turn loving-kindness into action — even toward a stranger we may never see again. When we help with humility, expecting nothing back, our kind act is pure. That is Mettā in motion: a promise kept, one small kindness at a time.

items: What does “Mara” stand for in our own lives? 「魔羅」代表我們生活中的什麼？
What does it mean to take Refuge? 「皈依」是什麼意思？
How can you turn Mettā into a real action this week? 你這週能如何把慈悲化為實際行動？

principle.lede: An intention is a seed; a kind act is the flower. This week we learn that a good plan only matters when we carry it into the world with a pure heart.
principle.h4: From intention to action 從發願到行動
principle.html: To set an intention is to point the heart in a wholesome direction (Right Thought). To take Refuge is to trust the Buddha’s teaching as our guide. But the path is finished by Mettā — loving-kindness made real. When you help a stranger with humility and want nothing in return, your kindness is pure, and you walk the Middle Path: full of effort, yet free of pride.

practice.note: Do one kind act for a stranger this week. First write your plan in the future tense; afterward, write your reflection in the past tense. Choose the tier that fits you. 先用未來式寫下計畫，做完後用過去式寫下反思。

tiers.req: Write 1 plan sentence with “I am going to…” and 1 reflection sentence with “I was…” or “I felt…”. Then say one good thing that happened today.
tiers.ex: ExampleI am going to smile at a stranger. I felt happy after I smiled.

tiers.req: Plan in 3–4 sentences using going to / will and a reason with because. Reflect in 2 sentences with feeling words. Then say whether your intention was pure Mettā.
tiers.ex: ExampleI am going to help someone carry their bag, because it is heavy. I will ask them in a kind voice. The stranger was surprised. I felt joyful because I showed kindness.

tiers.req: Plan in 5+ sentences with complex structures; you must use humility and Mettā. Reflect in 3–4 sentences on whether the act followed the Middle Path and how it strengthened your Right Thought.
tiers.ex: ExampleNext week, I am going to volunteer at a community center. I will approach the task with humility, remembering that the intention (Mettā) of this act is pure. Although the task was difficult, I found the Middle Path by not getting stressed. This act of Mettā reinforced my Right Thought.

quiz.text: Which word means “慈悲 / loving-kindness toward others”?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Compassion (慈悲, Mettā) is loving-kindness for others. Humility = 謙卑; stranger = 陌生人.

quiz.text: Complete the plan: “I ___ smile at a stranger tomorrow.”
quiz.expl: Correct: C. For a future plan we use “am going to + verb.” → I am going to smile.

quiz.text: Which sentence is a promise made in the moment (use “will”)?
quiz.expl: Correct: A. “Will” expresses a promise or decision made in the moment: I will help you.

quiz.text: To take Refuge (皈依) means to…
options: choose the Buddha’s teaching as your guide
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Refuge means setting a goal guided by the Buddha’s teaching — like a traveler following the North Star.

quiz.text: In the story, “Mara” stands for…
options: the doubts and troubles that test us
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Mara (魔羅) is a picture of life’s challenges — the doubts and fears that try to pull us off the path.

quiz.text: A kind act done with humility , expecting nothing back, is…
quiz.expl: Correct: A. When we help without wanting a reward, our intention is pure — this is Mettā in action.

quiz.text: Which word means “陌生人 / a person we do not know”?
quiz.expl: Correct: D. A stranger (陌生人) is someone we do not know — this week we offer one of them a kind act.

quiz.text: The Middle Path (中道) means effort that is…
options: as hard and tight as possible
steady — not too tight, not too loose
quiz.expl: Correct: B. The Middle Path is balanced: full of effort, yet free of strain and pride.

quiz.text: Add a reason: “I am going to help her ___ her bag is heavy.”
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Because introduces a reason: “…help her because her bag is heavy.”

quiz.text: What completes the lesson of this week best?
options: A good plan is enough on its own.
Kindness should always expect a reward.
We should only help people we already know.
An intention becomes real through a kind act.
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Refuge and intention are completed by Mettā — loving-kindness turned into real, humble action, even for a stranger.

title_zh: 正思惟 · 自我慈悲 · Right Thought / Self-Mettā 自我價值與真正的財富

pills.label: Principle
pills.text: Right Thought · Sammā Saṅkappa 正思惟

objectives.html: Use comparative adjectives (richer than, more important than) to compare and judge what truly matters, and build self-affirmations with “I am…”. Learn the vocabulary of value: self-worth, freedom, balance, humility, resilience.

Understand Right Thought (Sammā Saṅkappa) as choosing wholesome, kind thinking — including Self-Mettā, loving-kindness toward ourselves. Apply balance (Penhun 平衡) and the Middle Path, knowing wealth is a tool for serving others, not a goal.

examples.html: A calm heart is richer than gold.
examples.say: A calm heart is richer than gold.

examples.html: Friendship is happier than money.
examples.say: Friendship is happier than money.

examples.html: Working hard is better than being lazy.
examples.say: Working hard is better than being lazy.

examples.html: Love is more important than money.
examples.say: Love is more important than money.

examples.html: Kindness is more valuable than gold.
examples.say: Kindness is more valuable than gold.

examples.html: I matter. I am important.
examples.say: I matter. I am important.

examples.html: I am worthy of kindness.
examples.say: I am worthy of kindness.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Comparative adjectives
grammar.html: To compare two things, we use the comparative form plus than. For short words, add -er: rich → richer · happy → happier · hard → harder. For longer words, use more before the adjective: important → more important · valuable → more valuable. A few are irregular: good → better · bad → worse. Right Thought uses comparison to see what truly matters.

paras: Imagine two neighbors （兩位鄰居）. One has a large house full of gold, but he worries all night and never smiles. The other has a small house and little money, yet he sleeps in peace, shares his food, and laughs with his friends. Who is truly rich?

The Buddha taught that material wealth is only a tool — useful for helping others, but not the goal of life. The deepest wealth is a calm and kind heart. This is the Middle Path （中道） and balance (Penhun 平衡): we are not greedy, and we are not lazy. We work hard, and we also rest.

There is one more person we must remember to love — ourselves. Self-Mettā （對自己的慈悲） means turning loving-kindness inward. When a worried thought says “I am not good enough,” Right Thought answers gently: “I matter. I am more resilient than my worry. My value is greater than my mistakes.”

items: Which neighbor is truly rich, and why? 哪位鄰居真正富有？為什麼？
What does “balance (Penhun)” mean in daily life? 「平衡」在日常生活中是什麼意思？
What is one kind thing you can say to yourself? 你可以對自己說一句什麼善意的話？

principle.h4: Right Thought, the Middle Way 中道的正思惟
principle.html: Right Thought (Sammā Saṅkappa) means choosing thoughts of kindness, generosity, and non-harming — and that kindness includes ourselves. When negative self-talk appears, we do not fight it with more cruelty; we replace it with a true, gentle thought. To know our own worth is not arrogance (傲慢), and it is not low self-esteem either. It is the balance of the Middle Path: humble, kind, and quietly sure that we matter.

practice.note: Write in your journal every day this week. Each day, compare two values with a comparative adjective, and write a kind affirmation to yourself. Choose the tier that fits you. 每天比較兩種價值觀，並寫下一句對自己的肯定語。

tiers.req: Write 1 comparison using a simple comparative (better, happier) and 1 affirmation using “I am…”.
tiers.ex: ExampleA calm heart is better than a busy mind. I am important.

tiers.req: Write 2 comparisons with two different forms (more important, harder), comparing spiritual and material value, and 2 affirmations that say why you matter.
tiers.ex: ExampleKindness is more important than money. Working hard is better than being lazy. I am a good person because I am honest. I am important to my family.

tiers.req: Write 3–4 comparisons using complex structures to analyze the Middle Path or balance (Penhun), and 3 affirmations on how you use Right Thought to overcome anxiety or negative self-talk.
tiers.ex: ExampleLiving in humility is wiser than showing arrogance. Finding balance is harder than going to the extremes, but it provides more peace. When I feel anxiety, I remind myself, “I am more resilient than my worry.”

quiz.text: Which word means “自我價值 / how much we value ourselves”?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Self-worth (自我價值) is knowing that we matter. Humility = 謙卑; freedom = 自由.

quiz.text: Choose the correct comparative form of rich .
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Short adjectives add -er: rich → richer. We never say “more rich” or “more richer.”

quiz.text: Complete the comparison: “Love is ___ money.”
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Long adjectives use more … than: “Love is more important than money.”

quiz.text: Which sentence is a kind affirmation about yourself?
options: Money is the most important thing.
quiz.expl: Correct: B. An affirmation is a true, kind “I am…” sentence. “I am worthy of kindness” practices Self-Mettā.

quiz.text: In this lesson, who is truly rich ?
options: The person with the most gold
The person with the biggest house
The person with a calm and kind heart
The person who worries all night
quiz.expl: Correct: C. True wealth is a calm, kind heart — not gold. Material wealth is only a tool for helping others.

quiz.text: “Balance (Penhun 平衡)” on the Middle Path means…
options: not too greedy and not too lazy
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Balance avoids the extremes — we work hard and we rest, neither greedy nor lazy.

quiz.text: Which word means “謙卑 / not arrogant or proud”?
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Humility = 謙卑. Resilience = 韌性; freedom = 自由.

quiz.text: Self-Mettā (對自己的慈悲) means…
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Self-Mettā extends loving-kindness inward — being gentle and kind to yourself, not just others.

quiz.text: Which is the correct comparative of good ?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Good is irregular: good → better. “Working hard is better than being lazy.”

quiz.text: When a worried thought says “I am not good enough,” Right Thought answers…
options: “I am more resilient than my worry.”
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Right Thought replaces cruel self-talk with a true, gentle thought: my value is greater than my mistakes.

title_en: The Middle Path and Right Action
title_zh: 中道與正業 · Magga · The Path 八正道之道諦

pills.label: Principle
pills.text: The Middle Path · Right Action 正業

objectives.html: Use comparatives (more difficult, better than) and superlatives (the most important, the best) to compare and rank actions and values. Review the four main tenses through reflection.
Understand Right Action (正業) and Right Livelihood (正命), and use the Middle Path (中道) to evaluate behavior — choosing what is wholesome over what is unwholesome in daily life.

examples.html: Kindness is more important than money.
examples.say: Kindness is more important than money.

examples.html: Being honest is better than lying.
examples.say: Being honest is better than lying.

examples.html: Helping others is harder than ignoring them.
examples.say: Helping others is harder than ignoring them.

examples.html: The Middle Path is the best choice.
examples.say: The Middle Path is the best choice.

examples.html: Helping others is the most rewarding action I can take.
examples.say: Helping others is the most rewarding action I can take.

patterns.label: Pattern 3 · Explaining a wise choice
patterns.formula: I chose ___ because it is the most ___.

examples.html: I chose the best action because it avoided the extremes.
examples.say: I chose the best action because it avoided the extremes.

examples.html: Being a teacher is the most wholesome job because it helps people grow.
examples.say: Being a teacher is the most wholesome job because it helps people grow.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Comparatives & Superlatives
grammar.html: For short words, add -er to compare and -est for the best/most: kind → kinder → the kindest. For long words, use more and the most: important → more important → the most important. Some are irregular: good → better → the best · bad → worse → the worst. Use than with comparatives: better than lying.

paras: Before he was a teacher, the Buddha tried the path of extreme effort （苦行）. For years he ate almost nothing and pushed his body to the edge. He grew so thin and weak that he could barely think — yet he was no closer to peace.

One day he heard a music teacher on a boat speaking to a student about a lute （一種弦琴）. “If the string is too tight, it will snap. If the string is too loose, it will not play. Tune it just right, and it makes beautiful music.”

The Buddha understood at once. A life of harsh extremes is like a broken string; a lazy life is like a string too loose to sing. The wisest way is the Middle Path — not too tight, not too loose. From that balance grows Right Action: choosing what is wholesome and avoiding what causes harm. The best choice is rarely the most extreme one; it is the most balanced one.

items: What happens to a string that is too tight or too loose? 弦太緊或太鬆會怎樣？
What does “tuning the string just right” mean for our actions? 「調到剛剛好」對我們的行為是什麼意思？
Why is the most balanced choice usually the best choice? 為什麼最平衡的選擇通常是最好的選擇？

principle.lede: The Path (Magga) is the fourth Noble Truth — the way that leads out of suffering. Two of its steps are Right Action and Right Livelihood, and both are guided by the Middle Path.
principle.h4: Choosing the Wholesome 抉擇善業
principle.html: Right Action (正業) means not harming, not stealing, and acting with honesty and care. Right Livelihood (正命) means earning a living in a way that does not hurt other beings. When you face a choice, ask: Which action is the most wholesome? Which one keeps the balance of the Middle Path? The worst way to make money is one that harms others; the easiest way to be kind is to start with a single honest, helpful act today. Comparing actions wisely — better, best, most balanced — is how Right View becomes Right Action.

practice.note: Each day this week, compare and rank your choices to check that you are walking the Middle Path. Choose the tier that fits you. 每天用比較級和最高級評價你的行為，選一個適合自己的等級。

tiers.req: Write 2 sentences using the best and the worst to describe today. Add 1 sentence celebrating your best choice.
tiers.ex: ExampleThe best part of my day was laughing. The worst feeling was anger. I am happy because I made the best choice.

tiers.req: Write 2 sentences comparing a right action with an unwholesome one, then 1 sentence explaining why your best choice fits the Middle Path.
tiers.ex: ExampleBeing honest is better than lying. Helping others is the most rewarding action I can take. I chose the best action because it avoided the extremes of laziness and stress.

tiers.req: Write 3–4 sentences using superlatives to discuss Right Livelihood and Right Action with complex structures, then 2 sentences reflecting on your hardest-but-best choice.
tiers.ex: ExampleBecoming a teacher is the most wholesome job because it helps people grow. When I chose to tell the truth, it was the hardest decision, but it brought the most peace to my heart.

quiz.text: Which word means “有益的／良善的”?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Wholesome means good and beneficial (有益的). Unwholesome is the opposite (無益的).

quiz.text: Complete the comparison: “Kindness is ___ money.”
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Long adjectives use more … than: more important than. We do not say “importanter.”

quiz.text: Which is the correct superlative of good ?
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Good is irregular: good → better → the best.

quiz.text: What does Right Action (正業) mean?
options: earning as much money as possible
not harming, not stealing, acting with care
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Right Action means behaving without harming others — no stealing, honesty, and kindness in what we do.

quiz.text: In the story, the lute string teaches us that the wisest way is…
options: tuned just right — not too tight, not too loose
quiz.expl: Correct: D. The string must be tuned just right. This is the Middle Path — balance between two extremes.

quiz.text: Right Livelihood (正命) is about…
options: earning a living without harming other beings
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Right Livelihood means making a living in a way that does not hurt others — a wholesome job.

quiz.text: Choose the correct sentence to name the best choice.
options: The Middle Path is more best choice.
The Middle Path is the best choice.
The Middle Path is best than choice.
The Middle Path is the most best choice.
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Use the best on its own — never “the most best” or “more best.”

quiz.text: Which word means “無益的／有害的”?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Unwholesome (無益的) describes actions that cause harm — the opposite of wholesome.

quiz.text: Finish the sentence: “Helping others is the ___ action I can take.”
quiz.expl: Correct: A. A superlative needs the most with long adjectives: the most rewarding.

quiz.text: According to the Middle Path, the best choice is usually…
options: the one that earns the most money
the most balanced and wholesome one
quiz.expl: Correct: D. The Middle Path avoids extremes. The wisest action is the most balanced and wholesome one.

title_zh: 禪定與當下 · Right Mindfulness & Right Concentration · 正念與正定 (Sati / Samādhi)

pills.label: Principle
pills.text: Right Concentration · Samādhi 正定 · Sati 正念

pills.label: English
pills.text: Articles a / an / the · Prepositions in / on / at

objectives.html: Use articles correctly — a / an for general things and the for specific ones — and use prepositions of place and time (in, on, at, under) to say exactly where and when you feel calm.

Understand Right Concentration (Samādhi) and Right Mindfulness (Sati) as the fruit of steady Right Effort — learning that real peace is found not in a perfect place, but in the present moment, with the focus resting gently on the breath.

examples.html: I need a moment of peace.
examples.say: I need a moment of peace.

examples.html: The Buddha taught the Eightfold Path.
examples.say: The Buddha taught the Eightfold Path.

examples.html: A simple life is enough.
examples.say: A simple life is enough.

patterns.label: Pattern 2 · Prepositions of place — in / on / under

examples.html: Peace is in my heart.
examples.say: Peace is in my heart.

examples.html: My concentration is on my breath.
examples.say: My concentration is on my breath.

examples.html: I put the stress under a chair.
examples.say: I put the stress under a chair.

patterns.label: Pattern 3 · Prepositions of time — at / in / on

examples.html: I meditate at night.
examples.say: I meditate at night.

examples.html: I can find silence at any time.
examples.say: I can find silence at any time.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Articles and prepositions
grammar.html: Use a / an for something general (one of many) and the for something specific that we both know: a kind thought vs. the Middle Path. Use in for inside (in my heart), on for a surface (on my breath), and at for an exact place or time (at night). These tiny words help us point to where and when our calm lives.

paras: Imagine a child who cannot study because the room is noisy （房間很吵）. She runs to a quiet corner, but a dog barks. She runs to the garden, but the wind blows. Everywhere she goes, something disturbs her, and she grows tired and sad.

A wise teacher smiles and says, “You are looking for peace in a place. But peace does not live in a room or a corner — it lives in the present moment.” The teacher shows her how to bring her attention back, again and again, to one simple thing: her own breathing. This steady returning is Right Mindfulness (Sati), and the calm, gathered focus it brings is Right Concentration (Samādhi).

The last three steps of the Eightfold Path — Right Effort, Right Mindfulness, and Right Concentration — work together like climbing one stair at a time. With patient Right Effort, the child learns to rest her focus on her breath even in a noisy room. She discovers the secret: the key to peace is not the location, but the focus. The calm was never far away — it was always here, in this very moment.

items: Where did the child first look for peace? 小孩起初在哪裡尋找平靜？
What did the teacher say peace really lives in? 老師說平靜真正住在哪裡？
How can you bring your focus back to the present moment? 你如何把專注帶回當下？

principle.h4: Concentration, the Fruit of Effort 精進結出的果實
principle.html: Right Concentration is not forcing the mind to be still — it is the quiet gathering of attention that grows from kind, steady Right Effort and gentle Right Mindfulness. When your focus rests on your breath and your heart settles in the present moment, you can find calmness at any time and in any place, even a noisy one. Peace is not somewhere far away; it is here, now.

practice.note: Every day this week, use articles and prepositions to write down where and when you found your peace. Choose the tier that fits you. 每天用冠詞和介詞寫下你在哪裡、什麼時候找到平靜。

tiers.req: Write 2 preposition sentences using in and on to show where peace is, plus 1 affirmation using a or the.
tiers.ex: ExampleI feel calm in my bed. I see a book on a table. I am a peaceful student.

tiers.req: Write 3–4 sentences using in, on, at and the articles a, an, the to describe time and place, then say where your concentration is.
tiers.ex: ExampleI feel relaxed at night. I put the stress under a chair. I am reading an interesting book. My focus is on my breathing.

tiers.req: Write 4–5 sentences using complex structures and all the prepositions and articles. Reflect on how Right Mindfulness (Sati) helps you find calm in an imperfect place.
tiers.ex: ExampleI practice the Right Mindfulness even in a noisy room. The key to my peace is not the location, but the focus on my breathing. I can find silence at any time.

quiz.text: Which word means “正定 / calm, gathered focus”?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Concentration (專注力／正定) is the calm, gathered focus of Samādhi. Release = 釋放; cessation = 滅.

quiz.text: Choose the correct article: “I need ___ moment of peace.”
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Use a before a general singular noun beginning with a consonant sound: a moment.

quiz.text: Which sentence uses the correctly for a specific, known thing?
options: The Buddha taught a Eightfold Path.
The Buddha taught the Eightfold Path.
A Buddha taught a Eightfold Path.
quiz.expl: Correct: C. There is only one specific Eightfold Path, so we use the: “the Buddha taught the Eightfold Path.”

quiz.text: Choose the correct article: “I am reading ___ interesting book.”
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Use an before a vowel sound: an interesting book.

quiz.text: Which preposition fits: “Peace is ___ my heart.”
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Use in for something inside: in my heart.

quiz.text: Which preposition fits: “My concentration is ___ my breath.”
quiz.expl: Correct: C. We rest our focus on a surface or object of attention: on my breath.

quiz.text: Which preposition of time fits: “I meditate ___ night.”
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Use at with night and exact times: at night, at any time.

quiz.text: The last three steps of the Eightfold Path are Right Effort, Right Mindfulness, and…
quiz.expl: Correct: D. The final three steps are Right Effort (正精進), Right Mindfulness (正念 / Sati), and Right Concentration (正定 / Samādhi).

quiz.text: What does the teacher say is the real key to peace?
quiz.expl: Correct: A. “The key to my peace is not the location, but the focus.” Peace lives in the present moment, not in a perfect place.

quiz.text: Right Concentration (Samādhi) grows out of…
options: forcing the mind to be still
steady Right Effort and gentle Right Mindfulness
finding the quietest place in the world
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Samādhi is the fruit of kind, steady Right Effort and gentle Right Mindfulness — not force, and not a perfect place.

title_en: Sustained Effort and the Present Moment
title_zh: 持續精進與當下 · Viriya & Sati 正精進與正念

pills.label: Principle
pills.text: Right Effort · Viriya 正精進 · Mindfulness · Sati 正念

pills.label: English
pills.text: Adverbs of manner · the four main tenses

objectives.html: Form and use adverbs of manner by adding -ly (wise → wisely), including irregular ones (good → well, fast → fast). Review the four main tenses — simple present, present continuous, simple past, simple future — to describe when an action happens.

Understand Right Effort (Viriya) — preventing unwholesome states and cultivating wholesome ones — and define Mindfulness (Sati) as awareness of the present moment. Use the wisdom learned earlier to guide our effort, and act it out steadily, every day.

patterns.label: Pattern 1 · Saying how you act (adverb of manner)

examples.html: We speak truthfully.
examples.say: We speak truthfully.

examples.html: I always listen carefully.
examples.say: I always listen carefully.

examples.html: He acted wisely and kindly.
examples.say: He acted wisely and kindly.

examples.html: I am sitting quietly now.
examples.say: I am sitting quietly now.

examples.html: I am breathing slowly at this moment.
examples.say: I am breathing slowly at this moment.

examples.html: I will speak kindly next time.
examples.say: I will speak kindly next time.

examples.html: Yesterday, I responded thoughtfully instead of shouting.
examples.say: Yesterday, I responded thoughtfully instead of shouting.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Adverbs of manner & the four tenses
grammar.html: An adverb of manner tells us how we do something. Most are made with adjective + -ly: wise → wisely · calm → calmly · truthful → truthfully. Watch the irregular ones: good → well and fast → fast. Then choose the right tense to show when: simple present for habits (always, every day), present continuous for now (at the moment), simple past for reflection (yesterday), and simple future for plans (tomorrow, next week).

paras: Imagine a young student named Anya. Last week she learned to be wise. This week her teacher asks a new question: not only what is wise, but how do you act? Anya thinks for a moment and says, “I will act wisely.” The teacher smiles — Anya has turned an adjective into an adverb of manner, a word that shows how we do things.

One day Anya felt angry and wanted to shout. But she remembered Right Effort (Viriya): stop the unkind thing before it grows, and grow the kind thing instead （阻止惡、生起善）. So she breathed calmly and answered gently. “Last week I shouted,” she said. “Today I am speaking kindly, and tomorrow I will try again.” One sentence held the past, the present, and the future — and a steady, joyful effort.

Then her teacher asked her to sit quietly. Anya noticed her breath, the sounds in the room, the warm light. She was not thinking about yesterday or tomorrow. She was simply here. That is Mindfulness (Sati) （正念）: full, gentle awareness of the present moment. Effort and mindfulness work as one — we cultivate good habits steadily, and we abandon harmful ones, one mindful moment at a time.

items: How did Anya change “wise” into an adverb? 她如何把「wise」變成副詞？
What are the two sides of Right Effort? 正精進的兩個面向是什麼？
What does it mean to be mindful of the present moment? 「對當下保持正念」是什麼意思？

principle.lede: Right Effort is not a single burst — it is steady, conscious energy, repeated every day. Mindfulness is the gentle light that keeps that effort awake in the present moment.
principle.h4: Steady Effort, Awake Mind 持續的精進，清醒的心
principle.html: Right Effort (Viriya) has four sides: prevent unwholesome states from arising, abandon those that have arisen, cultivate wholesome states, and keep them growing. Notice that we describe this with adverbs — we act steadily, wisely, calmly — and we anchor it with tenses: a habit in the simple present, a mindful action in the present continuous. When we act wisely every day and stay aware right now, effort and mindfulness become one path that leads toward Concentration (Samādhi).

practice.note: Each day this week, record one act of Right Effort. Use an adverb to say how you acted, and a tense to show when. Choose the tier that fits you — the daily practice is the effort. 每天記錄一個正精進的行為，用副詞描述方式，用時態確認時間。

tiers.req: Write 2 sentences in the simple present, each with an adverb, about a kind daily habit. Then write 1 sentence in the simple past about a calm moment today.
tiers.ex: ExampleI always listen carefully. I speak kindly to my friend. I felt calm when I read quietly in my room.

tiers.req: Write 1 sentence in the present continuous with an adverb about your focus right now. Then write 2 sentences in the simple past with adverbs about a Right Effort that worked.
tiers.ex: ExampleI am sitting peacefully right now, focusing steadily on my breath. Yesterday, I responded thoughtfully instead of shouting. I finished my homework diligently.

tiers.req: Write 4–5 sentences combining future, past, and present continuous with several adverbs to discuss the four sides of Right Effort. Then reflect: how do effort and mindfulness help you overcome greed (貪) or anger (嗔)?
tiers.ex: ExampleI will try harder to speak gently and truthfully tomorrow. I am working patiently on my difficult assignment now, preventing frustration. Last week, I successfully avoided a mean comment, which I now see as acting wisely.

quiz.text: Make the adjective wise into an adverb of manner.
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Most adverbs of manner are made with adjective + -ly: wise → wisely (明智地).

quiz.text: An adverb of manner tells us…
quiz.expl: Correct: A. An adverb of manner describes how an action is done — for example, “She acted kindly.”

quiz.text: Which is the correct irregular adverb for good ?
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Good is an adjective; its adverb of manner is the irregular form well. (We also keep fast → fast.)

quiz.text: For a habit we do every day , which tense do we use?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. The simple present describes habits — the steady Right Effort we repeat every day.

quiz.text: To describe mindfulness right now , choose: “I ___ quietly at the moment.”
quiz.expl: Correct: C. The present continuous (am/is/are + -ing) shows what is happening now — perfect for Mindfulness (Sati).

quiz.text: Promise future effort: “I ___ kindly next time.”
quiz.expl: Correct: A. The simple future (will + verb) is for plans: “I will speak kindly next time.”

quiz.text: What does Right Effort (Viriya) mean?
options: working hard only before a test
stopping the unwholesome and cultivating the wholesome
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Right Effort prevents and abandons unwholesome states, and cultivates wholesome ones — steady, conscious energy.

quiz.text: Mindfulness (Sati) is best described as…
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Mindfulness is gentle, full awareness of the present moment (當下) — being here, now.

quiz.text: Which word means 培養 / to grow a good habit ?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Cultivate (培養) means to grow or develop. Its opposite here is abandon (捨棄), to let go.

quiz.text: Choose the sentence that joins a good adverb with a correct tense for a habit.
options: I am act wise every day.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. “I always act wisely” uses the simple present for a habit plus the adverb of manner wisely — Right Effort, every day.

title_en: The Eightfold Path Synthesis & Dialogue Practice
title_zh: 八正道總整理 · Speaking the Path 在正念對話中說出正道

pills.label: Principle
pills.text: The Path · Magga 道諦 · The Noble Eightfold Path

pills.label: English
pills.text: All Q1 grammar in mindful dialogue

objectives.html: Synthesize all Q1 grammar in conversation: four tenses (past, present, continuous, future), comparative and superlative adjectives, adverbs, articles, and prepositions. Practice a “seeking advice” dialogue, and learn review vocabulary: comprehensive, synthesis, resolve, challenge.

Summarize Quarter 1 through the Fourth Noble Truth: Magga 道諦. Name and describe the eight steps of the Noble Eightfold Path, and discuss how they work together to resolve the suffering (Dukkha 苦) we meet in daily life.

patterns.label: Pattern 1 · Naming a challenge (past & present continuous)

examples.html: I am feeling anxious because I have a test tomorrow.
examples.say: I am feeling anxious because I have a test tomorrow.

examples.html: I am feeling sad because I argued with my friend yesterday.
examples.say: I am feeling sad because I argued with my friend yesterday.

patterns.label: Pattern 2 · Giving advice with the Path (future + comparative)
patterns.formula: You can use ___. It is the ___ way.

examples.html: You can use Right Mindfulness. I am breathing calmly now.
examples.say: You can use Right Mindfulness. I am breathing calmly now.

examples.html: Right Speech is the kindest way. I will speak gently tomorrow.
examples.say: Right Speech is the kindest way. I will speak gently tomorrow.

patterns.label: Pattern 3 · Seeking-advice dialogue
patterns.formula: A: What should I do? · B: You can try ___.

examples.html: I am feeling stressed about my test. What should I do?
examples.say: I am feeling stressed about my test. What should I do?

examples.html: You can try Right Effort. Study a little every day, and you will feel calmer.
examples.say: You can try Right Effort. Study a little every day, and you will feel calmer.

examples.html: Thank you. That is the most helpful advice.
examples.say: Thank you. That is the most helpful advice.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Putting all the tenses together
grammar.html: In a real conversation we mix tenses naturally. We describe a problem with the past (I argued) or present continuous (I am feeling worried), and we offer hope with the future (You will feel better). Use comparatives to compare (a calmer way) and adverbs to say how (speak kindly, breathe slowly).

paras: The Buddha taught that when we feel suffering (Dukkha 苦), we do not have to stay stuck. There is a comprehensive way forward — the Noble Eightfold Path （八正道）. The eight steps are:

1. Right View 正見 — seeing things as they truly are. 2. Right Thought 正思惟 — kind and wise intentions. 3. Right Speech 正語 — speaking truthfully and gently. 4. Right Action 正業 — acting with care and not harming.

5. Right Livelihood 正命 — earning a living honestly. 6. Right Effort 正精進 — steady, joyful effort each day. 7. Right Mindfulness 正念 — staying aware in the present moment. 8. Right Concentration 正定 — a calm, focused mind.

No single step works alone. Right Effort needs Right Mindfulness; Right Speech needs Right Thought. Together they resolve the challenges of daily life — and this whole quarter, every grammar lesson has been one more tool to walk this path and talk about it.

items: How many steps are on the Noble Eightfold Path? 八正道有幾個步驟？
Which step helps you stay aware in the present moment? 哪一步幫你保持當下的覺察？
Why is it called a path and not just one rule? 為什麼叫做「道路」而不只是一條規則？

principle.lede: The Fourth Noble Truth tells us there is a way out of suffering — the Path (Magga 道諦). It has eight steps that work together, like eight strings on one instrument. Here is the whole path in simple words.
principle.h4: One Path, Eight Steps 一條道路，八個步驟
principle.html: The Path is the most complete way to find peace. The eight steps are not eight separate rules — they support one another like the eight spokes of a wheel. When a challenge comes, we do not panic; we ask, “Which step of the Path can I use right now?” That is how wisdom turns into action, and how the words we learn turn into a kinder, calmer life.

practice.note: Make a note or mind map that links each Quarter 1 Buddhist concept to its English grammar and one example sentence. Choose the tier that fits you, and get ready for next week’s Q1 assessment. 把每個佛學概念與對應的語法和例句連起來。

tiers.req: Link 3 concepts (e.g., Right Effort, Mettā, Dukkha) to a grammar point and write 1 example sentence for each.
tiers.ex: ExampleMettā → Adverbs → I speak kindly to my friend.

tiers.req: Link 5 concepts to grammar. Include at least Simple Past and Present Continuous, each with a clear example sentence.
tiers.ex: ExampleKarma → Simple Past → I slept well because I finished my work.

tiers.req: Map all 6 concepts (Right Effort, Mindfulness, Karma/Volition, Mettā, Dukkha, Magga) using comparatives and adverbs, then write a short reflection on the whole Path.
tiers.ex: ExampleSelf-Worth → Comparative Adjectives → My heart is more important than my phone.

quiz.text: How many steps are on the Noble Eightfold Path?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. The Noble Eightfold Path (八正道) has eight steps that work together.

quiz.text: Which word means “全面的 / covering everything”?
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Comprehensive means complete and covering everything (全面的).

quiz.text: Describe a problem in the present continuous: “I ___ worried about my test.”
quiz.expl: Correct: D. The present continuous is am/is/are + verb-ing → “I am feeling worried.”

quiz.text: Give hope with the future tense: “Study a little every day, and you ___ calmer.”
quiz.expl: Correct: B. The simple future is will + verb → “you will feel calmer.”

quiz.text: Which step of the Path helps you stay aware in the present moment?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Right Mindfulness (正念) keeps us aware and present, here and now.

quiz.text: Choose the correct comparative: “This is a ___ way to solve the problem.”
quiz.expl: Correct: A. For short adjectives we add -er: calm → calmer. Never use “more” with “-er.”

quiz.text: Pick the adverb to say how you speak: “I speak ___ to my friend.”
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Adverbs describe how we do something: speak kindly, breathe slowly.

quiz.text: In Buddhism, Dukkha (苦) means…
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Dukkha (苦) is the suffering or trouble we meet in life. The Path helps us resolve it.

quiz.text: What does the verb resolve mean?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. To resolve (解決) a challenge is to solve or settle it.

quiz.text: Why is the Eightfold Path called a “path,” not just one rule?
options: The eight steps work together to lead us to peace.
It must be walked outside on a real road.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. The eight steps support one another like spokes of a wheel — together they form the path that leads to peace.

title_zh: 情緒管理與捨 · Equanimity 平等心／捨心

objectives.html: Use descriptive adjectives for feelings (overwhelmed, calm, stressed) and action verbs for managing them (accept, let go, observe). Master the patterns “I can control…” and “I cannot control…”, plus “It is better to…”

Understand Upekkhā (equanimity, 捨心) as a balanced heart that is not controlled by strong emotion. Practice Right Action and Right Speech by focusing energy inside the Circle of Control — and meet every feeling with non-judgmental observation.

examples.html: I can control my attitude.
examples.say: I can control my attitude.

examples.html: I can control my words and my actions.
examples.say: I can control my words and my actions.

examples.html: I cannot control the weather.
examples.say: I cannot control the weather.

examples.html: I cannot control other people’s feelings, so I let go.
examples.say: I cannot control other people's feelings, so I let go.

examples.html: It is better to observe my anger than to act on it.
examples.say: It is better to observe my anger than to act on it.

examples.html: When I feel overwhelmed, it is better to breathe and accept.
examples.say: When I feel overwhelmed, it is better to breathe and accept.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · can / cannot for ability and choice
grammar.html: We use can and cannot (can’t) with the base verb to say what is — and is not — in our power. I can control my actions. · I cannot control the past. The verb never changes for he / she / it: She can let go. Notice that let go stays as two words: I let go of my worry.

paras: One day a student came to a teacher, upset. “Someone was rude to me,” she said, “and now I feel angry all day.” The teacher pointed to a still pond. “Throw a stone in,” he said. The student did, and the water churned and rippled.

“Now make the ripples stop with your hands,” the teacher said. The student splashed and pushed, but the water only grew wilder. So she stopped and waited. Slowly, on its own, the pond became calm and clear again.

“Your feelings are the ripples,” the teacher said. “You cannot control whether the stone is thrown — but you do not have to splash. Observe, accept, and let go, and your heart returns to stillness.” This balanced, unshaken heart is Upekkhā — equanimity. We cannot control every stone, but we can always control our attitude, our words, and our actions: our Circle of Control.

items: What do the ripples in the pond stand for? 池塘的漣漪代表什麼？
Why did splashing make the water worse? 為什麼用力撥水反而更亂？
Name one thing inside your Circle of Control today. 說出今天你「控制圈」裡的一件事。

principle.lede: Imagine your mind is a quiet pond. A big feeling drops in like a stone, and the water ripples. With equanimity, you simply watch the ripples until the water grows still again.
principle.h4: The Circle of Control 控制圈
principle.html: Equanimity is not coldness, and it is not pretending we have no feelings. It is the calm, balanced heart that observes a feeling without being swept away. We cannot control the weather, the past, or another person’s mood — and worrying about those things only makes the water wilder. But we can always control three things: our attitude, our words, and our actions. When we pour our effort into that small circle and let go of the rest, peace follows.

practice.note: Each day this week, write about one stressful moment. Sort it into what you can control and what you must let go of. 每天記錄一個壓力情境，分成「可控」與「放手」兩部分。

tiers.req: Write a situation (simple past), one “I can control…” sentence, and one “I cannot control…” sentence.
tiers.ex: ExampleMy friend was angry at me. I can control my words. I cannot control my friend’s feelings.

tiers.req: Describe a stressful situation. Write 2 “I can control…” sentences about your attitude and actions, then 1 sentence saying why you let go.
tiers.ex: ExampleI felt stressed because I failed the math test. I can control my attitude to stay positive. I will study harder next time. I cannot control the past, so I let go of my worry.

tiers.req: Analyze a complex situation involving others. In 3–4 sentences use observe, accept, detach to explain how you controlled your own reaction, then add a reflection.
tiers.ex: ExampleI observed my frustration, but I chose to accept the reality (Upekkhā). My words were encouraging instead of critical. Focusing on my own effort made the situation less stressful.

quiz.text: Which word means “平等心 / a calm, balanced heart”?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Equanimity (平等心／捨心, Upekkhā) is the calm, balanced heart. Overwhelmed and stressed are difficult feelings.

quiz.text: Complete the pattern: “I ___ control my attitude.”
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Your attitude is inside your Circle of Control, so we say “I can control my attitude.”

quiz.text: Which is something you cannot control?
quiz.expl: Correct: D. The weather is outside the Circle of Control. Your attitude, words, and actions are the three things you can control.

quiz.text: In the pond story, the ripples stand for our…
quiz.expl: Correct: B. The ripples are our strong feelings. We watch them settle instead of splashing and making them worse.

quiz.text: Why did splashing the water make the pond worse?
options: Fighting the feelings keeps the water moving.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Struggling against a feeling only stirs it up. Equanimity means we observe and let the water settle on its own.

quiz.text: What are the three things in your Circle of Control?
options: the weather, the past, and other people
your friends, your school, and your grades
your attitude, your words, and your actions
your luck, your mood, and the future
quiz.expl: Correct: C. We can always control our attitude, words, and actions — and pouring our energy there brings peace.

quiz.text: Which word means “觀察 / to watch a feeling without judging it”?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. To observe (觀察) is to watch a feeling without judging it — the heart of mindfulness and Upekkhā.

quiz.text: Which sentence correctly uses “It is better to…”?
options: It is better control my anger.
It is better to controlling my anger.
It is better to controls my anger.
It is better to observe my anger than to act on it.
quiz.expl: Correct: D. The pattern is “It is better to + (base verb).” → It is better to observe my anger.

quiz.text: Someone is rude to you. Which is the Upekkhā response?
options: Shout back to make them stop.
Observe your anger, accept it, and choose kind words.
Worry about it all day long.
Pretend you have no feelings at all.
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Equanimity is not coldness. We observe the feeling, accept it, and still choose Right Speech and Right Action.

quiz.text: What does “let go” mean in this lesson?
options: Try to control everything around you.
Stop worrying about what you cannot control.
Give up and do nothing at all.
quiz.expl: Correct: C. To let go (放下) is to release worry about what is outside your Circle of Control, so your energy stays on what you can change.

title_en: Emotional Regulation and the Power of Pause
title_zh: 情緒調節與停頓的力量 · Right Effort · Vīrya 正精進

pills.label: Principle
pills.text: Right Effort · Vīrya 正精進 · Nonreaction 不反應

pills.label: English
pills.text: “I am in control of…” · “When I feel X, I will Y.”

objectives.html: Use action verbs for managing emotions: stop, breathe, inhale, exhale, choose, manage. Master the affirmation “I am in control of my emotions / words / actions.” and the plan pattern “When I feel X, I will Y.” Describe feelings with adjectives like calm, stressed, overwhelmed.

Apply Right Effort (Vīrya 正精進) to transform and manage strong emotions. Understand and practice nonreaction (不反應) — choosing calm instead of reacting — and cultivate the inner peace that prepares the heart for Ahimsa (non-violence).

patterns.label: Pattern 1 · Taking control
patterns.formula: I am in control of my ___.

examples.html: I am in control of my emotions.
examples.say: I am in control of my emotions.

examples.html: I am in control of my words and my actions.
examples.say: I am in control of my words and my actions.

examples.html: When I feel angry, I will stop and breathe.
examples.say: When I feel angry, I will stop and breathe.

examples.html: When I feel stressed, I will inhale slowly and exhale.
examples.say: When I feel stressed, I will inhale slowly and exhale.

patterns.label: Pattern 3 · Choosing nonreaction & strategies
patterns.formula: Instead of ___, I choose to ___. / I manage stress by ___.

examples.html: Instead of arguing, I choose to stop and breathe.
examples.say: Instead of arguing, I choose to stop and breathe.

examples.html: I manage stress by listening to music.
examples.say: I manage stress by listening to music.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · “by” + verb-ing for methods
grammar.html: To say how we do something, we use “by + verb-ing.” I manage stress by playing sports. · She calms down by reading. Notice the -ing ending after by. With “When I feel X, I will Y,” we use the simple present after when and will for the plan: When I feel overwhelmed, I will breathe slowly.

paras: Imagine a small pond in a quiet garden. When the water is still, you can look down and see everything clearly — the stones, the fish, the sky reflected like a mirror. This is the mind that is calm （平靜的心，像平靜的池塘）.

Now imagine a child throws a stone into the pond. Splash! The water jumps, ripples spread everywhere, and the surface becomes messy. Suddenly you cannot see clearly at all. A strong feeling — anger, stress, or being overwhelmed — is just like that stone. It makes the mind react, and everything becomes cloudy.

But here is the secret: you do not have to throw another stone back. You can stop. You can inhale slowly, and exhale slowly. You can focus on your heartbeat and wait. Little by little the ripples fade, and the water becomes still again. This waiting — not reacting — is called nonreaction （不反應）. With the steady power of Right Effort (Vīrya), you choose calm, and you see clearly once more.

items: What happens to the pond when you throw in a stone? 丟石頭進池塘會發生什麼事？
What is the “stone” in our minds? 我們心中的「石頭」是什麼？
How can you make the water calm again? 你要如何讓水再次平靜？

principle.lede: Strong feelings are not “bad.” The skill is the pause between the feeling and what we do next — Stop, Breathe, Choose.
principle.h4: Mindful restraint, the Middle Way 中道的克制
principle.html: Right Effort (Vīrya 正精進) is the steady energy we use to calm a stormy mind. When a strong feeling arrives, we do not push it down by force, and we do not let it explode. We simply stop, breathe (inhale and exhale slowly), and then choose a kind response. This is nonreaction (不反應): the small, brave act of not throwing the stone back. Controlling our own anger is already a quiet act of Ahimsa — non-violence toward ourselves and others — which is exactly where next week’s path begins.

practice.note: Each day this week, write about one moment when you felt a strong emotion, and how you used Stop, Breathe, Choose to practice nonreaction. Choose the tier that fits you. 每天記錄一次強烈情緒，並寫下你如何「停、吸、選」。

tiers.req: Write a situation (1 sentence), an action using stop and breathe (1 sentence), and the affirmation (1 sentence).
tiers.ex: ExampleI felt angry at my toys. I stopped and took a deep breath. I am in control of my hands.

tiers.req: Describe a situation that needed nonreaction (1 sentence), explain the calm strategy you chose (2 sentences), and add a control sentence with “I am in control of…”
tiers.ex: ExampleMy friend was rude to me in class. I chose nonreaction instead of arguing. I decided to listen to music to calm down. I am in control of my feelings and my voice.

tiers.req: Analyze a complex, overwhelming situation (1 sentence), then write a Vīrya response (3–4 sentences) explaining your breathing technique and how this control links to Ahimsa. Note your personal strategy.
tiers.ex: ExampleWhen I felt overwhelmed by school deadlines, I wanted to yell. I used Right Effort to stop myself. I inhaled for four counts and exhaled for six. Controlling my anger is a small act of Ahimsa toward my family. Singing a happy song helped me feel calm again.

quiz.text: Which word means “吸氣 / to breathe in”?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Inhale = to breathe in (吸氣). Exhale = to breathe out (呼氣).

quiz.text: Complete the affirmation: “I ___ in control of my emotions.”
quiz.expl: Correct: A. The pattern is “I am in control of ___.” Use am with I.

quiz.text: What does nonreaction (不反應) mean?
options: staying calm and not reacting right away
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Nonreaction means we pause and choose calm instead of reacting — not the same as hiding or running away.

quiz.text: Finish the plan: “When I feel angry, I will ___.”
quiz.expl: Correct: B. After “I will” we use the base verb: stop and breathe. This is the calm choice.

quiz.text: Choose the correct way to say your method.
options: I manage stress by playing sports.
I manage stress by play sports.
I manage stress for playing sports.
I manage stress to play sports.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. The pattern is “by + verb-ing”: by playing sports.

quiz.text: In the story, the calm pond is like a mind that is…
quiz.expl: Correct: C. When the pond is still, you can see clearly — that is the calm, clear mind.

quiz.text: In the story, the “stone” thrown into the pond stands for…
options: a strong feeling like anger or stress
quiz.expl: Correct: B. A strong feeling is like a stone that makes the mind ripple and lose its clarity.

quiz.text: “Right Effort” (Vīrya) in this lesson means…
options: pushing your feelings down by force
steady energy to stop, breathe, and choose calm
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Vīrya is the steady, kind effort to pause and choose a calm response — the Middle Way.

quiz.text: Which word means “不知所措的 / having too much to handle”?
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Overwhelmed (不知所措的) means a feeling is too big to handle. Calm is the opposite.

quiz.text: Why is controlling your anger a small act of Ahimsa (non-violence)?
options: Because it makes you win every argument.
Because it lets you ignore other people.
Because anger is always a good thing.
Because it stops harsh words and harm to yourself and others.
quiz.expl: Correct: D. When we pause instead of reacting, we prevent harsh words and harm — a quiet step toward Ahimsa.

title_zh: 非暴力的實踐 · Non-Violence of Body, Speech & Mind 身、語、意的不害

objectives.html: Use the caring pattern “I care about…” and the rule pattern “Ahimsa means no…” and “I will not…” to describe gentle choices. Learn vocabulary: Ahimsa, compassion, non-harm, master, gentle.

Understand Ahimsa (non-violence) in body, speech, and mind. Learn to “wash away” angry thoughts with breath and mindfulness, and see how one unkind thought can grow — so we choose to be the master of our own mind.

examples.html: I care about you.
examples.say: I care about you.

examples.html: I care about my family.
examples.say: I care about my family.

examples.html: I care about the world and all living things.
examples.say: I care about the world and all living things.

patterns.label: Pattern 2 · Naming the rule of Ahimsa

examples.html: Ahimsa means no fighting.
examples.say: Ahimsa means no fighting.

examples.html: Ahimsa means no screaming or bad words.
examples.say: Ahimsa means no screaming or bad words.

examples.html: I will not fight or push my sister, even when I am angry.
examples.say: I will not fight or push my sister, even when I am angry.

examples.html: I am the master of my thoughts.
examples.say: I am the master of my thoughts.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · “will not” for promises
grammar.html: We use will not (or won’t) to make a promise about the future: I will not scream. · I won’t use bad words. After “no” in “Ahimsa means no ___,” we use the -ing form: no fighting, no screaming. Practicing Ahimsa means choosing gentle words again and again.

paras: Long ago a kind sage found a frightened little mouse （老鼠） running from a cat. Feeling compassion, the sage used his power to turn the mouse into a strong cat. But then a dog chased the cat, so the sage turned it into a dog. When a tiger frightened the dog, the sage made it into a tiger. At last, to give the creature a peaceful life, he turned the tiger into a man.

The man lived well — but slowly an unkind thought grew in his mind: “I am better than the sage. Without him I would still be powerful. I do not need to be grateful.” The thought became pride, and the pride became cruelty. The man even wished harm upon the very sage who had saved him.

Seeing the harm inside the man’s heart, the sage said gently, “You have forgotten kindness — so you may return to what your heart has become.” In a moment the man was a small mouse again. One bad thought, left to grow, had cost him everything. This is why we practice Ahimsa in the mind: we notice an unkind thought early, breathe, and wash it away before it grows. We choose to be the master of our thoughts.

items: How did the sage show compassion to the mouse? 智者如何對老鼠表現慈悲？
What thought grew inside the man’s mind? 那個人心中長出了什麼念頭？
What can you do when an unkind thought begins to grow? 當不善的念頭開始生長，你可以怎麼做？

principle.lede: Ahimsa is non-violence in three places at once. We keep our body gentle, our speech kind, and our mind peaceful.
principle.h4: Body, Speech & Mind 身、語、意
principle.html: Ahimsa in the body means I do not fight, push, or hurt — not people, not animals, not living things. Ahimsa in speech means I do not scream or use bad words, even when I lose a game. Ahimsa in the mind means I notice an angry thought and, like water in a waterfall, I wash it away and fill my heart with love and joy. When I master one small unkind thought today, I keep my whole world gentle. That is real strength.

practice.note: This week, write about a time you felt angry or sad — and how you used Ahimsa to calm down. You may draft in Chinese first, then write it in English. 本週寫下一次生氣或難過的經驗，以及你如何用「不害」讓自己冷靜。可先用中文打草稿，再寫成英文。

tiers.req: Write 1 sentence using “I care about…” or “I will not…” plus a vocabulary word. Name one gentle choice.
tiers.ex: ExampleI care about my sister. I will not push her.

tiers.req: Write 3–4 sentences about a time you felt angry. Say what happened, and how you calmed down using Ahimsa. Use a “when” clause.
tiers.ex: ExampleI lost a video game and felt angry. I wanted to scream, but I stopped. When I breathed slowly, I felt peaceful. I will not use bad words.

tiers.req: Write 3 ways you will practice Ahimsa at home — one for the body, one for speech, one for the mind. Reflect on the story of the mouse.
tiers.ex: ExampleBody: I will not fight my brother. Speech: I will not scream when I lose. Mind: I will stop the thought “I hate this” and change it to “I will breathe and calm down.”

quiz.text: What does Ahimsa mean?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Ahimsa means non-violence (非暴力／不害) — being gentle in body, speech, and mind.

quiz.text: Ahimsa is practiced in three places. Which three?
quiz.expl: Correct: A. True non-violence lives in the body, the speech, and the mind (身、語、意).

quiz.text: Complete the caring sentence: “I ___ my family.”
quiz.expl: Correct: D. The pattern is “I care about ___.” → I care about my family.

quiz.text: Choose the correct rule sentence.
quiz.expl: Correct: B. After “no” we use the -ing form: Ahimsa means no fighting.

quiz.text: Which is an example of Ahimsa in speech ?
options: I will not push my friend.
I will breathe and calm my thoughts.
I will not scream or use bad words.
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Ahimsa in speech means kind words — no screaming and no bad words.

quiz.text: In the Waterfall meditation, we imagine water that helps us…
quiz.expl: Correct: A. We picture the waterfall to wash away negativity and fill the heart with love and joy.

quiz.text: In the story, why did the man turn back into a mouse?
options: The sage ran out of power.
One unkind thought grew until he became cruel.
quiz.expl: Correct: D. One bad thought, left to grow, became pride and cruelty — so his heart returned to a mouse.

quiz.text: To master your thoughts means to…
options: stay in gentle control of your mind
quiz.expl: Correct: B. To master (掌握／控制) your thoughts is to stay calmly in control — “I am the master of my thoughts.”

quiz.text: Make a gentle promise. Which sentence is correct?
options: I will not fight my brother.
I not will fight my brother.
I will not fighting my brother.
I am not fight my brother.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. A promise uses “will not + base verb” → I will not fight.

quiz.text: Why do violent video games go against Ahimsa?
