options: They are too easy to win.
They take a long time to play.
They cost a lot of money.
They feed thoughts of harming instead of caring.
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Ahimsa widens our care for all life; violent games train the mind toward harm, which pulls the heart the other way.

title_en: Mettā — Good Will for All Life
title_zh: 慈心的善意 · Loving-Kindness 慈心

objectives.html: Use the well-wishing pattern “May you be happy and healthy” and “I send love to…” to name the people you care for. Learn kind-heart vocabulary: loving-kindness, good will, universal, recipient, and the Pali word Mettā.

Understand Mettā (Loving-Kindness) as good will — wishing others well. See that love is universal and crosses every boundary, and practice a daily guided meditation to strengthen the “good-will muscle.”

examples.html: May you be happy and healthy.
examples.say: May you be happy and healthy.

examples.html: May you be safe and peaceful.
examples.say: May you be safe and peaceful.

examples.html: May all beings be happy.
examples.say: May all beings be happy.

examples.html: I send love to my family.
examples.say: I send love to my family.

examples.html: I send love to my classmates and to the animals.
examples.say: I send love to my classmates and to the animals.

patterns.label: Pattern 3 · What I am doing now (present continuous)

examples.html: I am sending good will to everyone on Earth.
examples.say: I am sending good will to everyone on Earth.

examples.html: I am breathing slowly and sending kindness from my heart.
examples.say: I am breathing slowly and sending kindness from my heart.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · “May you…” & the present continuous
grammar.html: The modal verb “may” is used to make a gentle wish for someone: May you be happy. We do not add -s after may. We also use the present continuous (am / is / are + verb-ing) to say what is happening right now: I am sending love.

paras: Imagine the kindness in your heart as a small, warm light. （想像你心中的慈愛是一道溫暖的光。） When you practice Mettā, that light grows brighter and spreads outward in circles of love.

The first circle is the smallest — it is you. You say, “May I be happy and healthy.” You cannot give away a light you do not first hold. The next circle is your family, then your friends and classmates, then the animals and all of nature.

The last circle is the biggest of all: everyone on Earth — even strangers, and even people we find hard to like. （甚至包括陌生人，以及我們不喜歡的人。） Mettā is universal, because our happiness and the happiness of all beings are interconnected. Loving-kindness is for everybody.

items: Who is in the very first circle of love? 第一個愛的圈圈裡是誰？
Why does the last circle include people we don’t like? 為什麼最後一個圈圈也包含我們不喜歡的人？
What does it mean that love is “universal”? 愛是「普遍的」是什麼意思？

principle.lede: In the guided meditation, place your hand on your heart and repeat: “May you be happy, may you be healthy.” Then think about how good will can be trained, like a muscle, a little every day.
principle.h4: Good Will, a Friendly Heart 善意的友善心
principle.html: Mettā is not only a feeling — it is a steady attitude of friendliness toward all life. When we stop harming others (Ahimsa), we fill that space with active kindness. Through Bhāvanā (training the mind), we make the heart used to producing kind thoughts. The more you practice loving-kindness, the stronger your good-will muscle grows — and you learn to rejoice in the happiness of every living being.

practice.note: For the next 7 days, do the “Hand on Heart” meditation for 2 minutes each morning, then write one journal line. Choose the tier that fits you. 每天早上練習兩分鐘，再寫下慈心日記。

tiers.req: Write 1 sentence naming today’s recipient: “Today, I sent love to ______.” Then say the wish out loud.
tiers.ex: ExampleToday, I sent love to my mom. I said: “May you be happy and healthy.”

tiers.req: Write 3–4 sentences. Name two recipients from different circles, use “I send love to…”, and add the “May you be…” wish.
tiers.ex: ExampleToday, I sent love to my family and to the street cats. I send love to people I have never met. May you all be happy and healthy.

tiers.req: Write 5+ sentences. Include someone you find difficult to like, explain why love is universal, and reflect on how good will is interconnected with your own happiness.
tiers.ex: ExampleToday I am sending good will to a classmate I argued with. Although it is hard, I know my peace and his peace are interconnected. May you be safe; may you be peaceful.

quiz.text: What does the Pali word Mettā mean?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Mettā (慈心) is the Buddhist word for loving-kindness — a heart of good will toward all life.

quiz.text: Finish the wish: “May you ___ happy and healthy.”
quiz.expl: Correct: C. After “May you…” we use the base verb be: “May you be happy and healthy.”

quiz.text: Which sentence correctly names who you care for?
options: I send love to my family.
I sending love to my family.
I love send to my family.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. The pattern is “I send love to ___.” Don’t forget the word to.

quiz.text: “Good will” means…
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Good will (善意) is the warm wish that others be well and happy.

quiz.text: If love is universal , it is for…
options: only people who are kind to me
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Universal (普遍的) means for everybody — even strangers and people we find hard to like.

quiz.text: A recipient is…
options: the person you are sending love to
quiz.expl: Correct: A. A recipient (接受者) is the one who receives — here, the person you send your loving-kindness to.

quiz.text: In the Circles of Love, who is in the very first circle?
quiz.expl: Correct: D. The first circle is you. You cannot give away a light you do not first hold.

quiz.text: Choose the correct present-continuous sentence.
options: I sending good will to everyone.
I am sending good will to everyone.
I sends good will to everyone.
I send-ing good will to everyone.
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Present continuous = am / is / are + verb-ing: “I am sending good will.”

quiz.text: During the meditation, where do you place your hand?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. You place your hand on your heart and repeat, “May you be happy, may you be healthy.”

quiz.text: Why does the Teacher’s Note say loving-kindness is “like schoolwork”?
options: The more you practice, the stronger it gets.
It is boring and hard to finish.
You only need to do it once.
It is only for very young children.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Good will is like a muscle — through daily practice (Bhāvanā), the heart grows stronger at producing kind thoughts.

title_zh: 善良與同體大悲 · Love is Beautiful: Why We Care for All Beings 愛是美好的：為什麼我們要關愛眾生

pills.label: English
pills.text: “We should…” · “May all beings be free from…”

objectives.html: Use “should” to talk about what is right: “We should love everyone because we are all human.” Learn words for caring and for suffering: kind, mean, connected, suffering, ill-will. Practice the well-wish pattern “May all beings be free from…”

Understand interconnectedness (緣起 · Pratītyasamutpāda): helping others helps yourself. Extend loving-kindness beyond friends to strangers and animals — a first taste of 同體大悲, the equality of all beings.

examples.html: We should love everyone because we are all human.
examples.say: We should love everyone because we are all human.

examples.html: We should be kind because everyone wants to be happy.
examples.say: We should be kind because everyone wants to be happy.

examples.html: We should not hurt others, because we are all connected.
examples.say: We should not hurt others, because we are all connected.

patterns.label: Pattern 2 · Sending well-wishes
patterns.formula: May all beings be free from ___.

examples.html: May all beings be free from anger.
examples.say: May all beings be free from anger.

examples.html: May all beings be free from jealousy.
examples.say: May all beings be free from jealousy.

examples.html: May you be free from mental and physical suffering.
examples.say: May you be free from mental and physical suffering.

patterns.label: Pattern 3 · Saying no to harm (negative imperatives)

examples.html: No hitting, no screaming, no hurting others.
examples.say: No hitting, no screaming, no hurting others.

examples.html: We should not hurt animals.
examples.say: We should not hurt animals.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · The helping verb “should”
grammar.html: We use should to talk about the right thing to do. After should, always use the plain (base) verb: We should help. · We should be kind. For the negative, add not: We should not hurt others. Notice that should never adds -s, even for he or she: She should be kind.

paras: Imagine a great web （一張大網） stretched across the whole world. At every knot of the web there is a person, an animal, a tree. The threads connect them all. When one thread is pulled, every other thread feels the gentle tug. Nothing in the web is truly alone.

One day a child was unkind to another child — she said mean words and made him cry. But the web was connected, so the unkindness traveled along the threads. The first child felt heavy and unhappy too, because hurting others is hurting ourselves （傷害他人就是傷害自己）.

The next day she chose differently. She helped her friend, shared her food, and was gentle with the class cat. This time kindness traveled along the threads, and warmth came back to her own heart. We all eat, sleep, and wish to be happy — we are made of the same needs. When we care for any being, we care for the whole web, and for ourselves. That is the meaning of interconnectedness.

items: What does the great web stand for? 這張大網代表什麼？
Why does hurting someone else also hurt us? 為什麼傷害別人也會傷到自己？
How can you send kindness along the web this week? 這週你可以怎麼把善意傳遞出去？

principle.lede: All beings share the same needs and the same wish to be free from suffering. Because we are connected, kindness is not only good for others — it is the natural way to care for ourselves and the whole world.
principle.h4: One Body, Great Compassion 同體大悲
principle.html: In Buddhism, Pratītyasamutpāda (緣起) teaches that everything arises together — nothing stands apart. When we understand this, compassion is not a chore but common sense: a person who hurts and screams brings ill-will back into their own heart, while a person who shares kindness lives in peace. To bow with hands together (合十) is to say, “I see the goodness in you, and we belong to one another.” Loving strangers and animals is the first step of 同體大悲 — the great compassion that holds all beings as one body.

practice.note: Show your love this week, then write about it. Choose the tier that fits you — kindness in action is the lesson. 這週實際付出善意，再寫下來。選一個適合自己的等級。

tiers.req: Fill in the blanks, then do one kind act. Use the words Human, Animals, Peace, Connected.
tiers.ex: ExampleWe should not hurt Animals. I wish for everyone to live in Peace. I helped my mom today.

tiers.req: Write two short paragraphs: (1) Why is love important? (2) How will you show love this week? Use should and because.
tiers.ex: ExampleLove is important because it makes the world happy. We should be kind because we are all connected. I will show love by being kind to my cat.

tiers.req: Write a full “Love is Beautiful” essay (5+ sentences) and add four well-wishes using “May all beings be free from…” Reflect on interconnectedness.
tiers.ex: ExampleBecause we are all connected, my kindness returns to me. May all beings be free from anger, jealousy, and mental and physical suffering.

quiz.text: Which word means “刻薄的 / unkind and hurtful”?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Mean describes someone who is unkind or hurtful (刻薄的). Kindness is the opposite.

quiz.text: Complete the sentence: “We ___ love everyone because we are all human.”
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Use should + base verb: “We should love everyone.” Should never adds -s and never takes to.

quiz.text: Which sentence sends a kind well-wish correctly?
options: May all beings be free from anger.
May all beings is free from anger.
All beings free from anger may.
May all beings be free of angry.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. The pattern is “May all beings be free from ___ (noun).” Use be, from, and the noun anger.

quiz.text: In our story, the great web stands for…
quiz.expl: Correct: C. The web shows interconnectedness — pull one thread and every other thread feels it. We are all connected.

quiz.text: Why does the lesson say hurting others also hurts us?
options: Because it is against the rules
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Interconnectedness (緣起) means unkindness travels along the web and returns to our own heart.

quiz.text: Which word means “嫉妒”?
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Jealousy = 嫉妒. Suffering = 痛苦; peace = 和平.

quiz.text: Choose the correct way to say no to harm.
options: No hitting, no screaming, no hurting others.
No hit, no scream, no hurt others.
No to hit, no to scream, no to hurt.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. After no we use the -ing form: “No hitting, no screaming, no hurting others.”

quiz.text: Why are all people “connected,” according to the lesson?
options: Because we all live in one city
Because we all eat, sleep, and wish to be happy
Because we all have the same name
quiz.expl: Correct: C. We share the same basic needs (food, sleep) and the same wish to be happy — so we are connected as one body.

quiz.text: What does bowing with hands together (合十) mean?
options: “I am tired and want to rest.”
“I see the goodness in you; we belong to one another.”
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Añjali Mudrā (合十) is a gesture of peace and respect — recognizing the goodness in every heart.

quiz.text: What is the main lesson of “Love is Beautiful”?
options: Caring for all beings — even strangers and animals — also helps ourselves.
We should only love our best friends.
Being mean is the fastest way to win.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Because we are all connected, extending kindness to every being — including strangers and animals — brings peace back to our own heart.

title_en: The Heart of Love and Kindness
title_zh: 愛是一種情感：把善良種在心裡 · My Tree of Love 我的愛心樹

pills.label: Principle
pills.text: Right Speech · Inherent Light 正語·內在光明

pills.label: English
pills.text: “I love my ___ because…” · “An emotion is a feeling.”

objectives.html: Tell the difference between an emotion and a feeling, and use the adjectives generous, respectful, nice. Practice the pattern “I love my ___ because…” and simple Subject + Verb + Object sentences like “I respect all people.”

Understand that love is a clean emotion that rises from within. Practice Right Speech (正語) — never speaking badly of others behind their backs — and rest in the inherent light (內在光明), the bright Buddha-nature in every heart.

patterns.label: Pattern 1 · Saying why you love someone

examples.html: I love my mom because she is kind.
examples.say: I love my mom because she is kind.

examples.html: I love my grandpa because he listens to me.
examples.say: I love my grandpa because he listens to me.

examples.html: I love my friend because she is generous.
examples.say: I love my friend because she is generous.

examples.html: Love is an emotion.
examples.say: Love is an emotion.

examples.html: An emotion is a feeling in the heart.
examples.say: An emotion is a feeling in the heart.

examples.html: I respect all people.
examples.say: I respect all people.

examples.html: I am generous, and I share with my friends.
examples.say: I am generous, and I share with my friends.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Subject + Verb + Object & defining nouns
grammar.html: Many English sentences follow the order Subject + Verb + Object: I (subject) respect (verb) all people (object). To define a word, we use the verb is: An emotion is a feeling. Remember the article: a feeling, an emotion (use an before a vowel sound).

paras: Imagine that inside every person there is a small, warm light （內在光明）. The Buddha taught that this light — our bright, kind nature — is always there, even when we forget it. It never goes out.

How do we keep our light shining? One simple way is Right Speech （正語）. This means we do not say unkind things about people behind their backs. When we are respectful with our words, others feel safe with us, and trust grows. Trust is the strong root that makes a friendship — a relationship — last.

Being generous keeps the light bright too. Helping your grandparents, listening carefully when someone speaks, or sharing your spare coins are all small gifts of love (Dāna 布施). Love is an emotion, and kindness is what we do with it.

items: What is the difference between an emotion and a feeling? 情感和感覺有什麼不同？
How does Right Speech help build trust? 「正語」如何建立信任？
What is your “inherent light,” and how can you keep it shining? 你的「內在光明」是什麼？要如何讓它持續發亮？

principle.lede: Love is not only a word we say — it is an emotion that rises from inside us, and it shows up in how we speak and act toward others.
principle.h4: Let Your Light Shine 讓你的光明照耀
principle.html: Right Speech and generosity are not rules from outside — they are the natural way our inner light wants to act. Every time you choose a kind word over an unkind one, or share instead of keeping everything for yourself, your tree of love grows a little stronger. Love begins in the heart, and kindness carries it into the world.

practice.note: Draw a large tree. On the branches, draw leaves or hearts and write English words for love and kindness. Color your tree beautifully! Choose the tier that fits you. 畫一棵大樹，在枝葉上寫下表示愛與善良的英文字，選一個適合自己的等級。

tiers.req: Write at least 5 words on your tree, such as Love, Kind, Mom, Dad, Nice. Draw yourself at the bottom.
tiers.ex: SentenceLove is from my heart.

tiers.req: Write at least 8 words, including new vocabulary like Generous, Respect, Care, Helpful, Listen, Family, Friend, Share. Add one full sentence at the bottom.
tiers.ex: SentenceI show love by helping my grandma.

tiers.req: Write 10+ words (e.g. Relationship, Emotion, Inherent Light, Trust, Community, Harmony) plus a short reflection of 3–4 sentences.
tiers.ex: PromptWhy are respect and trust important in a friendship?

quiz.text: Which word means “慷慨的 / willing to give and share”?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Generous (慷慨的) describes someone who likes to give and share. Respectful = 尊重的; nice = 友善的.

quiz.text: Complete the definition: “An emotion ___ a feeling.”
quiz.expl: Correct: A. To define a single noun we use is: “An emotion is a feeling.”

quiz.text: Choose the correct Subject + Verb + Object sentence.
quiz.expl: Correct: D. English order is Subject (I) + Verb (respect) + Object (all people).

quiz.text: Finish the pattern: “I love my brother ___ he helps me.”
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Because gives the reason: “I love my brother because he helps me.”

quiz.text: “Right Speech” (正語) means that we…
options: talk as loudly as we can
do not say unkind things about people behind their backs
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Right Speech means guarding our words — not gossiping or speaking badly of others — so that trust can grow.

quiz.text: In this lesson, the “inherent light” (內在光明) is…
options: the bright, kind nature inside every person
a lamp on the teacher’s desk
quiz.expl: Correct: A. The inherent light is our Buddha-nature — the bright, kind heart that is always there and never goes out.

quiz.text: Which word means “信任 / believing someone is safe and true”?
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Trust (信任) is the strong root of a good relationship.

quiz.text: Helping your grandparents or sharing your coins is an example of…
options: generosity — a small gift of love (Dāna)
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Giving and helping is generosity (Dāna 布施) — kindness in action.

quiz.text: Choose the correct article: “Love is ___ emotion.”
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Use an before a vowel sound: “Love is an emotion.” (Compare: a feeling.)

quiz.text: What is the main lesson of “The Heart of Love and Kindness”?
options: Love is an emotion from the heart, and kindness is how we show it.
It is fine to gossip about friends.
We should keep our gifts to ourselves.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Love rises from within; with Right Speech, respect, and generosity, we let that light shine and our tree of love grows.

title_zh: 慈到悲 · Compassion 願世間無苦

pills.label: English
pills.text: “May you be free from pain.” · “I am thankful for…”

objectives.html: Learn the words Karuṇā (compassion) and gratitude, and the phrasal verb “let go.” Use the imperative wish pattern “May you be free from pain.” and the pattern “I am thankful for…” Review the adjectives perfect, respected, kind.

See how Mettā (慈) grows into Karuṇā (悲): when loving-kindness deepens, it becomes the wish to remove suffering. Understand “We are one” (同體) and how gratitude widens the heart so there is more room for compassion.

examples.html: May you be free from pain.
examples.say: May you be free from pain.

examples.html: May you be held in compassion.
examples.say: May you be held in compassion.

examples.html: May you know peace.
examples.say: May you know peace.

examples.html: I am thankful for my family.
examples.say: I am thankful for my family.

examples.html: I am thankful for my pets and my school.
examples.say: I am thankful for my pets and my school.

examples.html: I let go of my bad feelings.
examples.say: I let go of my bad feelings.

examples.html: When I let go, my heart feels light. We are one.
examples.say: When I let go, my heart feels light. We are one.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Adjective review
grammar.html: Adjectives describe people and things. This week we review perfect, respected, and kind: a kind heart, a respected teacher, a perfect moment. Notice the gentle wish word “May”: it does not give an order — it sends a blessing, as in May you be free from pain.

paras: One quiet morning, the Buddha sat with his students beside a still pond. He picked up a single small stone and dropped it into the water. Plip. Where the stone touched the water, a tiny circle appeared （漣漪）.

The little circle grew into a bigger circle, and then a bigger one, spreading out until the gentle ripples reached every edge of the pond. “One small stone,” the Buddha said, “touched the whole pond. Your kindness is like this stone.”

When you help one person who is sad or hurt, that person feels less suffering. Then they are kinder to someone else, and that person is kinder again. Loving-kindness (Mettā) wishes others happiness; compassion (Karuṇā) wishes that no one suffers — and like ripples, even a small act of compassion can change the whole world. Because we are all interconnected, we are one.

items: What did the small stone make in the water? 小石頭在水中造成了什麼？
How is your kindness like the stone? 你的善意如何像那顆石頭？
What is the difference between Mettā and Karuṇā? 「慈」與「悲」有什麼不同？

principle.lede: Mettā says, “I want you to be happy.” Karuṇā goes one step deeper and says, “I do not want you to suffer.” This week we let loving-kindness grow into compassion for the whole world.
principle.h4: Wishing No Suffering for the World 願世間無苦
principle.html: When the heart fills with gratitude, it becomes soft and wide — and a wide heart has more room for others. From that softness, Mettā (wishing happiness) naturally grows into Karuṇā (wishing the end of suffering). To “let go” of our own bad feelings is the first act of compassion: it heals our own heart so we can hold others gently. And because every life is interconnected, one small kind act ripples outward. Be the ripple that starts the change — for we are all one.

practice.note: This week, send your compassion into the world like ripples. Choose the tier that fits you. 選一個適合自己的等級，把慈悲化為行動。

tiers.req: Draw one person or animal you want to help. Then copy and complete these two sentences in your notebook.
tiers.ex: ExampleKaruṇā means compassion. I want my dog to be free from pain.

tiers.req: Use these 4 words in your own sentences: kindness, let go, suffering, heart. Then write about one time you helped someone who was sad or hurt.
tiers.ex: ExampleMy sister was crying. I gave her a hug. That is Karuṇā.

tiers.req: Write a short paragraph (5–6 sentences) about the Buddha’s story of the ripples. Explain how one small act of kindness can change the world, using interconnected, suffering, and peace.
tiers.ex: ExampleBecause we are all interconnected, helping a stranger reduces suffering and spreads peace like ripples.

quiz.text: Which word means “慈悲／悲心 / compassion”?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Karuṇā is the Pali word for compassion (悲心) — the wish that others be free from suffering.

quiz.text: Complete the wish: “May you ___ free from pain.”
quiz.expl: Correct: A. The wish pattern is “May you be free from ___.” After May you, we use the base verb be.

quiz.text: What does Mettā mean?
options: I do not care about you.
I want you to be happy.
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Mettā is loving-kindness: “I want you to be happy.” Karuṇā goes deeper: “I do not want you to suffer.”

quiz.text: Choose the correct sentence to give thanks.
options: I am thankful of my family.
I am thankful for my family.
quiz.expl: Correct: D. The pattern is “I am thankful for ___.” Don’t forget the word for.

quiz.text: In the story, the small stone in the pond stands for our…
quiz.expl: Correct: B. One small stone makes ripples across the whole pond — just as one small act of kindness can change the world.

quiz.text: The phrasal verb “let go” (放下) means to…
quiz.expl: Correct: C. To let go is to release bad feelings so the heart is not hurt — an act of self-compassion.

quiz.text: Which word means “痛苦／受苦”?
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Suffering = 痛苦／受苦. Karuṇā is the wish to remove others’ suffering.

quiz.text: “We are one” (We are interconnected) teaches us that…
options: we should think only of ourselves
our actions touch everyone, like ripples
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Because every life is interconnected, one kind act ripples outward and touches the whole world.

quiz.text: Which adjective correctly describes a teacher we look up to?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Respected is an adjective for someone we admire and honor. Perfect and kind are also review adjectives this week.

quiz.text: How does Mettā grow into Karuṇā?
options: Wishing happiness deepens into wishing the end of suffering.
By keeping all our gifts for ourselves.
By ignoring people who are sad.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. When loving-kindness (wishing happiness) deepens, it becomes compassion — the wish that no one should suffer.

title_en: Mindfulness and the Four Noble Truths
title_zh: 四聖諦 · Blowing Away the Bubbles 吹散念頭氣泡

pills.label: Principle
pills.text: Mindfulness · Sammā Sati 正念 · The Four Noble Truths 四聖諦

pills.label: English
pills.text: “When I am…, I will…” · future tense with “will”

objectives.html: Use the pattern “When I am ___, I will ___.” with the future tense “will” to state an intention, and learn calm-mind vocabulary: meditation, calm, focus, suffering, cause, enlightenment, inhale, exhale.

Practice Mindful Looking (Sammā Sati 正念) as a form of meditation, and meet the Four Noble Truths (四聖諦): suffering, its cause, its end, and the path — knowing that we can find peace even when life is hard.

examples.html: When I am angry, I will breathe slowly.
examples.say: When I am angry, I will breathe slowly.

examples.html: When I am worried, I will blow the thought bubble away.
examples.say: When I am worried, I will blow the thought bubble away.

examples.html: When I am distracted, I will bring my mind back.
examples.say: When I am distracted, I will bring my mind back.

examples.html: Meditation helps me with my homework.
examples.say: Meditation helps me with my homework.

examples.html: Meditation helps me pay attention.
examples.say: Meditation helps me pay attention.

patterns.label: Pattern 3 · Calling your mind back
patterns.formula: My mind is wandering. I will bring it back.

examples.html: Focus on the blue firefly.
examples.say: Focus on the blue firefly.

examples.html: Inhale, exhale, and blow the bubble away.
examples.say: Inhale, exhale, and blow the bubble away.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Future tense with “will” for intention
grammar.html: We use will + (base verb) to say what we plan or choose to do: I will meditate to be calm. · When I am sad, I will breathe. The verb after will never changes — it stays simple: I will focus, she will focus, they will focus. This is a promise to your own calm mind.

paras: 1. Suffering (苦) — Life has hard moments. Not getting what you want, or getting what you do not want, hurts. （苦：得不到想要的，就會痛苦。）
2. The Cause (因) — Much of this pain comes from wanting things very strongly or disliking things very strongly. （集：痛苦來自貪愛與排斥。）
3. The End (滅) — Peace is possible. Even when we are hurt, a calm mind can rest. The bubbles can stop growing. （滅：放下執著，平安是可能的。）
4. The Path (道) — We live each day with peace and wisdom — being kind, paying attention, and breathing through the hard times. （道：以平安與智慧而活，就是覺悟之路。）

items: What is the First Noble Truth about? 第一聖諦在說什麼？
What causes a lot of our suffering? 什麼是造成痛苦的「因」？
How can breathing help when a worry-bubble grows? 當憂慮的氣泡變大時，呼吸如何幫助你？

principle.lede: Mindfulness means watching the mind with kindness. When a busy thought rises like a bubble, we do not fight it — we notice it, breathe, and let it float away. With a calm and focused mind, the Buddha invited us to look honestly at life through the Four Noble Truths.
principle.h4: You are the master of your thoughts 你是念頭的主人
principle.html: The Four Noble Truths are not sad news — they are a map. They tell us that suffering is real, but it is not the end of the story: there is a way to peace. Each time you stop, inhale, exhale, and blow a thought bubble away, you take a small step on the path to enlightenment. The same kindness you practiced in earlier weeks (Karuṇā and Mettā) is part of this same path.

practice.note: Practice mastering your mind every day this week. Choose the tier that fits you — stopping to breathe is the practice. 每天練習，選一個適合自己的等級。

tiers.req: Draw a picture of yourself meditating. Draw 3 bubbles above your head with 3 things that make you happy. Then trace and complete two sentences.
tiers.ex: Trace“Meditation helps me be calm.” · “I am on the path.”

tiers.req: Think of a time you felt sad or angry this week. Write 3 sentences using the “When I am ___, I will ___.” structure.
tiers.ex: ExampleWhen I have too much homework, I will breathe to be calm. When I am sad, I will blow away the thought bubbles.

tiers.req: Write a short paragraph (5–8 sentences) explaining the Four Noble Truths in your own words, and how meditation helps you find peace in daily life.
tiers.ex: PromptWhy can we have peace even when we are suffering? How does breathing help you find the “End of Suffering” every day?

quiz.text: Which word means “禪修／冥想”?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Meditation (禪修／冥想) is the practice of calming and focusing the mind. Suffering = 苦; wander = 分心.

quiz.text: Complete the intention: “When I am angry, I ___ breathe slowly.”
quiz.expl: Correct: A. We use will + base verb to state an intention: “I will breathe slowly.”

quiz.text: In our practice, a “thought bubble” is something you should…
quiz.expl: Correct: C. We inhale, exhale, and blow the bubble away — we notice the thought kindly and let it float off, instead of letting it grow.

quiz.text: Which sentence uses “will” correctly for the future?
options: She will focuses on her work.
She will to focus on her work.
She will focusing on her work.
She will focus on her work.
quiz.expl: Correct: D. After will, the verb stays in its simple base form: will focus — never will focuses or will to focus.

quiz.text: The First Noble Truth tells us that…
options: suffering is a real part of life
homework is the cause of everything
quiz.expl: Correct: C. The First Noble Truth (苦) says suffering is real — not getting what we want, or getting what we don’t want, hurts.

quiz.text: According to the Second Noble Truth, much of our suffering comes from…
options: wanting or disliking things very strongly
quiz.expl: Correct: A. The Second Noble Truth (因/集) points to strong desire and dislike as a cause of much of our pain.

quiz.text: Which two words are opposites about breathing?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Inhale = 吸氣 (breathe in) and exhale = 呼氣 (breathe out). They are opposites.

quiz.text: When your mind drifts during practice, you can say…
options: “My mind is perfect, so I will stop.”
“My mind is wandering; I will bring it back.”
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Mindfulness is gentle. When the mind wanders, we kindly notice and bring it back — again and again.

quiz.text: What does the Third Noble Truth (the End) teach us?
options: Peace is possible, even when we are hurt.
quiz.expl: Correct: C. The Third Noble Truth (滅) gives us hope: a calm mind can rest in peace even in hard moments.

quiz.text: The class affirmation this week is…
options: “I am on the path to enlightenment!”
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Each kind, mindful step is part of the journey — so we say together, “I am on the path to enlightenment!”

title_zh: 隨喜 · Sympathetic Joy · 我為你感到高興

objectives.html: Use the social affirmations “I’m happy for you!” and “Good job!” to celebrate others. Learn the vocabulary Muditā, jealousy, antidote, empathetic, enough, and practice the affirmation “I am enough.”

Understand Muditā (隨喜) as the antidote to jealousy and competition. Learn interconnectedness — that someone else’s happiness does not make us smaller. Build inner stability with self-love so we can truly rejoice for others.

examples.html: I’m happy for you!
examples.say: I’m happy for you!

examples.html: I’m happy for my friend. She won the game.
examples.say: I’m happy for my friend. She won the game.

examples.html: Good job! That is wonderful news.
examples.say: Good job! That is wonderful news.

examples.html: I am safe. I am loved.
examples.say: I am safe. I am loved.

examples.html: I am happy. I am enough.
examples.say: I am happy. I am enough.

examples.html: They are safe. They are loved.
examples.say: They are safe. They are loved.

examples.html: They are happy, and I am happy for them.
examples.say: They are happy, and I am happy for them.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · “for” after happy, and the verb “to be”
grammar.html: To celebrate a person, we say I’m happy for you — don’t forget the word for. For affirmations we use the verb to be: I am enough for myself, and they are enough for others. Saying it kindly, out loud, is Right Speech.

paras: Imagine two children in the same class. Both worked hard, but only one of them won the drawing contest. The first child has a jealous heart （嫉妒的心） — small, dark, and tight. It says, “So what? Why not me?” That tight feeling does not take the prize away from anyone; it only makes the jealous child unhappy.

The second child has a Muditā heart （隨喜的心） — bright, open, and growing wider. It says, “Good job! I’m happy for you!” Nothing was lost. The winner is still the winner — but now two children are smiling instead of one. The joy doubled.

This is the secret of Muditā: another person’s success does not make you smaller. Jealousy is a little poison, and Muditā is the antidote （解毒劑）. Before we can be glad for others, we settle our own heart with self-love: I am safe, I am loved, I am happy, I am enough. When you know you are enough, your heart has plenty of room for everyone’s happiness.

items: What does a “jealous heart” feel like? 「嫉妒的心」是什麼感覺？
Why is Muditā called the antidote to jealousy? 為什麼說隨喜是嫉妒的解毒劑？
What can you say when a friend wins? 當朋友贏的時候，你可以說什麼？

principle.lede: When something good happens to a classmate, two hearts are possible. Notice which one you grow.
principle.h4: When your friend wins, you win too 隨喜的美好
principle.html: Muditā is one of the Four Immeasurables (四無量心), alongside loving-kindness (Mettā) and compassion (Karuṇā). It is the practice of rejoicing in others’ good fortune instead of comparing ourselves to them. Remember: you are enough, you are loved, and your heart has plenty of room for everyone’s happiness. Good job, class!

practice.note: This week, take the “Good Job” Challenge: find one person — a friend, sibling, or parent — who is happy or successful at something, and tell them “I’m happy for you” or “Good job!” Choose the tier that fits you. 選一個適合自己的等級。

tiers.req: Draw a picture of a friend winning a game or doing something well. Write 1 sentence, and copy the 3 words: Muditā, happy, love.
tiers.ex: ExampleI am happy for my friend.

tiers.req: Describe a moment this week when you saw someone else happy. Use the prompt below, and use the word enough in a sentence about yourself.
tiers.ex: ExampleMy sister was happy because she passed her test. I said: “Good job!” It made me feel proud. I am enough.

tiers.req: Write a short paragraph (5–8 sentences) explaining why Muditā is the antidote to jealousy. Why is it sometimes hard to be happy for others, and how does Muditā help our hearts grow bigger?
tiers.ex: ExampleIt is hard to be happy for others when I am comparing myself to them. But practicing Muditā reminds me that I am enough, so a friend’s success cannot make me smaller.

quiz.text: What does Muditā (隨喜) mean?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Muditā is sympathetic joy — being happy when good things happen to other people.

quiz.text: Which word means “嫉妒”?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Jealousy (嫉妒) is the tight, unhappy feeling Muditā helps us overcome.

quiz.text: Your friend wins a prize. What is a Muditā response?
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Muditā celebrates others. We say “Good job!” and “I’m happy for you!”

quiz.text: Choose the correct sentence to celebrate a friend.
quiz.expl: Correct: D. The pattern is “I’m happy for ___.” Don’t forget the word for.

quiz.text: An antidote (解毒劑) is something that…
options: cures or cancels out a poison
quiz.expl: Correct: C. An antidote cures a poison. Muditā is the antidote to the “poison” of jealousy.

quiz.text: Complete the self-love affirmation: “I am safe, I am loved, I am happy, I am ___.”
quiz.expl: Correct: A. The affirmation ends with “I am enough.” Knowing you are enough makes room to rejoice for others.

quiz.text: When you feel happy for someone else, the joy in the world…
options: grows bigger — now two people are glad
is taken away from the winner
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Muditā doubles the joy: the winner is still glad, and now you are glad too.

quiz.text: An empathetic person is one who…
options: can feel and share what others feel
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Empathetic (感同身受的) means you can feel along with others — the heart of Muditā.

quiz.text: Does a friend’s success make you smaller?
options: No — there is enough good fortune for everyone.
Yes — only one person can be happy.
Yes — you must beat them next time.
No, because you should hide your feelings.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Another person’s success does not make you smaller. Muditā lets both of you share the joy.

quiz.text: Muditā belongs to a group of practices called the…
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Muditā is one of the Four Immeasurables (四無量心), with loving-kindness (Mettā) and compassion (Karuṇā).

title_zh: 喜悅從家開始：感激父母無私的愛 · Joy with My Family

objectives.html: Use the cause-and-effect pattern “I appreciate my parents because…” to give reasons, and learn character vocabulary: appreciate, selfless, perseverance, altruistic, provide. Review the simple past tense for telling stories.
Understand that the practice of Muditā (隨喜) begins at home. Learn the importance of gratitude (報恩) and filial piety (孝道), and see that true wealth is not money but love and compassion.

examples.html: I appreciate my parents because they take care of me.
examples.say: I appreciate my parents because they take care of me.

examples.html: I appreciate my grandma because she is selfless.
examples.say: I appreciate my grandma because she is selfless.

examples.html: I appreciate my dad because he works hard for our family.
examples.say: I appreciate my dad because he works hard for our family.

patterns.label: Pattern 2 · Sharing joy without envy
patterns.formula: I am not jealous; I am happy for ___.

examples.html: My friend won the race. I am not jealous; I am happy for her.
examples.say: My friend won the race. I am not jealous; I am happy for her.

examples.html: My brother got a good grade, and I am happy for him.
examples.say: My brother got a good grade, and I am happy for him.

patterns.label: Pattern 3 · How parents care for us (simple present)

examples.html: My parents provide food and a home for me.
examples.say: My parents provide food and a home for me.

examples.html: Love is more important than money.
examples.say: Love is more important than money.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Simple past for storytelling
grammar.html: When we retell a story, we use the simple past tense. Many verbs add -ed: My friend played well. Others change form: My friend won the race. · I felt happy for her. Use the past tense to share a moment of Muditā that already happened.

paras: There was once a boy whose father was a poor farmer （一位貧窮的農夫）. The other children at school had new shoes and shiny lunchboxes, but the boy had only old clothes. He felt ashamed of his father, and he wished his family were rich.

What the boy did not know was that his father quietly gave away almost everything. When a neighbor’s child was hungry, the father shared their rice. When an old man could not walk, the father carried him home. He never spoke about it, and he never asked for thanks. His love was selfless （無私的） — and altruistic （利他的）.

One day the boy fell sick. Many people from the village came to help, because they remembered the father’s kindness. The boy finally understood: his family was not poor at all. His father had given them the greatest wealth — a heart full of love. From that day, the boy appreciated his father, and his envy turned into joy.

items: Why was the boy ashamed at first? 男孩一開始為什麼覺得羞愧？
What kind, selfless things did the father do? 父親默默做了哪些無私的善事？
What is the “greatest wealth” the story talks about? 故事中所說「最大的財富」是什麼？

principle.lede: Muditā means “having joy with others.” It is the opposite of envy — and the place to begin practicing it is right where we live.
principle.h4: Joy with My Family 喜悅從家開始
principle.html: It is sometimes easiest to feel joy for strangers and hardest to feel it for the people closest to us. Muditā begins at home: being truly happy when your brother does well, thanking your parents for a love that asks for nothing back. When you let go of jealousy and appreciate the selfless care your family gives, your whole house fills with light. As the teacher reminds us, “When you are happy for your parents and your siblings, your whole house fills with joy.” Be a joy-sharer this week.

practice.note: “Muditā Starts at Home.” Choose the tier that fits you, and practice being a joy-sharer with your family this week. 選一個適合自己的等級，本週在家中練習隨喜。

tiers.req: Draw a picture of your mom or dad doing something for you (cooking or driving). Then trace and complete the sentences. Smile and say “Thank you” to your parents 3 times this week.
tiers.ex: ExampleI love my family. Thank you for taking care of me.

tiers.req: Write 3 reasons why you appreciate your parents or guardians. Use the word selfless to describe someone in your family.
tiers.ex: ExampleI appreciate my parents because they cook for me, help me study, and never give up. My grandma is selfless because she always shares her food.

tiers.req: Write a paragraph (6–10 sentences) about the father’s story. Why did the son change his mind? Then name one moment this week when you felt jealousy, and describe how you turned it into Muditā.
tiers.ex: ExampleAt first the son was ashamed, but he learned that his father’s selfless love was true wealth. When my friend won, I felt envy — then I chose to be happy for her instead.

quiz.text: What does Muditā mean?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Muditā (隨喜) means having joy with others — being happy when good things happen to them.

quiz.text: Which word means “感激 / to be thankful for”?
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Appreciate means to value and be thankful for something. Envy = 妒忌; provide = 養育.

quiz.text: Choose the correct sentence to give a reason.
options: I appreciate my parents they help me.
I appreciate my parents because they help me.
quiz.expl: Correct: D. The pattern is “I appreciate ___ because ___.” A reason must follow because.

quiz.text: A selfless person is someone who…
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Selfless (無私的) means putting others before yourself — like the father in our story.

quiz.text: Your friend wins a race. What is the Muditā response?
options: “I wish I had won, not you.”
“I am not jealous; I am happy for you.”
“I do not care about the race.”
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Muditā is the opposite of envy — we feel pure, jealous-free joy for another person’s success.

quiz.text: Tell the story in the past tense. Which is correct?
options: My friend won the race, and I felt happy.
My friend win the race, and I feel happy.
My friend winned the race, and I feeled happy.
My friend wins the race, and I am happy.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. For storytelling we use the simple past: win → won, feel → felt.

quiz.text: In the story, why did the boy feel ashamed at first?
options: His father gave away the family’s rice.
He lost a race at school.
His family was poor and he wished they were rich.
quiz.expl: Correct: D. The boy was ashamed of being poor and wished his family were rich — until he understood his father’s love.

quiz.text: According to this lesson, true wealth is…
options: a big house and new shoes
quiz.expl: Correct: B. The story teaches that real wealth is not money but love and compassion — “Love is more important than money.”

quiz.text: Complete the sentence: “My parents ___ food and a home for me.”
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Provide (養育) means to give what is needed: parents provide for us.

quiz.text: Where does the teacher say Muditā should start?
options: at home, with your own family
quiz.expl: Correct: D. “Muditā is a beautiful light that starts in your own home.” Be happy for your parents and siblings first.

title_zh: 我的心、靈與能量：在第三眼尋找專注 · My Heart, My Mind, My Energy

objectives.html: Use rich adjectives to describe inner experience: foggy, wonderful, complete, peaceful. Practice the patterns “Meditation helps me with…” and “I feel… after meditation.” Speak affirmations using “I am…”.

Understand Samādhi (concentration, 定) as the calm focus that clears the “foggy” mind. Learn the Third Eye as a point of focus, connect meditation with positive energy, and build healthy self-worth through identity affirmations rooted in Buddha-nature.

examples.html: Meditation helps me with my homework.
examples.say: Meditation helps me with my homework.

examples.html: Meditation helps me with my sleep.
examples.say: Meditation helps me with my sleep.

examples.html: Meditation helps my brain not feel foggy.
examples.say: Meditation helps my brain not feel foggy.

patterns.label: Pattern 2 · Describing how you feel after

examples.html: I feel wonderful after meditation.
examples.say: I feel wonderful after meditation.

examples.html: I feel calm and complete after meditation.
examples.say: I feel calm and complete after meditation.

examples.html: When I focus on my third eye, I feel peaceful.
examples.say: When I focus on my third eye, I feel peaceful.

examples.html: I am love. I am at peace.
examples.say: I am love. I am at peace.

examples.html: I am enough. I am complete.
examples.say: I am enough. I am complete.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Adjectives after “feel” and “am”
grammar.html: After feel and am we use an adjective — a describing word — not a verb. I feel wonderful. · I am complete. Try to grow past the word “good”: use wonderful, amazing, peaceful, complete to paint a clearer picture of your energy.

paras: Imagine a glass of water with mud stirred inside it. The water looks brown and foggy （霧濛濛的）. You cannot see through it. This is like a busy, worried mind — full of thoughts about tests, games, and worries, all swirling at once.

Now set the glass down and keep it perfectly still. Do not touch it. Slowly, the mud sinks to the bottom, and the water becomes clear. This stillness is Samādhi （定） — the calm focus we practice in meditation. Our 15 minutes of sitting is the time we let the mud settle.

When the mind is clear, a quiet light shines from inside. We point our attention to the Third Eye （第三眼，眉心）, the soft spot between the eyebrows, and we feel a warm, bright energy. Then we speak gently to ourselves: “I am enough. I am complete.” A clear mind knows it is already whole.

items: What makes the water in the glass become clear? 玻璃杯裡的水為什麼會變清澈？
Where is the Third Eye, and why do we focus there? 第三眼在哪裡？我們為什麼專注在那裡？
What does it mean to say “I am complete”? 說「我是圓滿的」是什麼意思？

principle.lede: As we grow older, we can sit a little longer. A longer meditation builds Vīrya (energy and will) and a deeper Samādhi (concentration). Together they turn a foggy mind into a clear, bright one.
principle.h4: Your Energy Is Your Light 你的能量就是你的光
principle.html: When you meditate, you clear away the fog so your light can shine bright. A “foggy brain” feels confused and tired; a “clear mind” feels calm, focused, and full of positive energy. By repeating affirmations like “I am enough, I am complete, I am loved,” you are not pretending — you are recognizing your own Buddha-nature, the goodness already complete inside you. Remember: you are enough, you are complete, and you are loved.

practice.note: Write about your experience with the 15-minute meditation. You may write in English, or draw and use key words. Choose the tier that fits you. 寫下你 15 分鐘禪修的體驗，可用英文，也可畫圖加關鍵字。

tiers.req: Draw yourself meditating with a full heart. Copy these 3 words: Heart, Mind, Energy. Then trace and complete the sentence.
tiers.ex: ExampleI feel wonderful after meditation.

tiers.req: Write 3–4 sentences about how meditation helped you this week. Use a feeling word and the words third eye and positive energy.
tiers.ex: ExampleMeditation helps me with my sleep. When I focus on my third eye, I feel calm. I have positive energy. I am complete.

tiers.req: Write a short paragraph (6–10 sentences) on the connection between meditation and energy. Explain why a clear mind beats a foggy brain, and how affirmations change your energy when you are stressed.
tiers.ex: ExampleWhen my brain is foggy, I cannot focus at school. After fifteen minutes of meditation, my mind feels clear and my energy is wonderful. Saying “I am enough” reminds me that I am already complete.

quiz.text: Which word means “能量 / the power inside you”?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Energy (能量) is the bright power we feel after meditation. Foggy = unclear; complete = whole.

quiz.text: A foggy mind feels…
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Foggy (霧濛濛的) means unclear, like muddy water. Meditation clears the fog so the mind becomes bright.

quiz.text: Complete the sentence: “I feel ___ after meditation.”
quiz.expl: Correct: D. After feel we use an adjective. Wonderful describes the feeling — better than just “good.”

quiz.text: Where is the Third Eye ?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. The Third Eye (第三眼) is the soft spot between the eyebrows — our point of focus in meditation.

quiz.text: Choose the correct affirmation.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. The pattern is “I am + adjective.” → I am complete. We need the word am.

quiz.text: Pick the best sentence: meditation helps you.
options: Meditation helps me with my homework.
quiz.expl: Correct: C. The pattern is “Meditation helps me with ___.” Don’t forget with.

quiz.text: In the story, the muddy water becomes clear when we…
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Stillness lets the mud settle. This is Samādhi (定) — calm focus that makes the mind clear.

quiz.text: Instead of just saying “Good,” try a stronger word like…
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Right Effort (正精進) includes using positive, high-energy words: wonderful, amazing, great.

quiz.text: When we say “I am enough,” we are recognizing our…
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Affirmations point to Buddha-nature (佛性) — the goodness and wholeness already complete inside you.

quiz.text: Why do we sit a little longer (15 minutes) as we grow?
options: to feel more tired and foggy
to build deeper concentration and energy
quiz.expl: Correct: C. A longer meditation builds Vīrya (energy/will) and deeper Samādhi (concentration) — a clearer, brighter mind.

title_zh: 禪修是我的良藥：移動我的能量 · Meditation is My Medicine

pills.label: Principle
pills.text: Meditation as Medicine · Self-Reliance 自依止

pills.label: English
pills.text: “My ___ felt wonderful/excellent.” · “I am shaping my energy.”

objectives.html: Use stronger feeling words — wonderful and excellent — instead of plain “good.” Pair words with movement: heart, mind, energy. Build the pattern “My ___ felt wonderful/excellent.” and say “I am shaping my energy.”
Understand meditation as medicine — an active way to heal the mind. Learn moving meditation through Tai-Chi-style gestures, and take responsibility for your own peace (self-reliance · 自依止).

patterns.label: Pattern 1 · Describing a feeling with stronger words

examples.html: My mind felt excellent after school today.
examples.say: My mind felt excellent after school today.

examples.html: My energy was wonderful when I helped my mom.
examples.say: My energy was wonderful when I helped my mom.

examples.html: My heart felt wonderful after I meditated.
examples.say: My heart felt wonderful after I meditated.

examples.html: I am shaping my energy with my hands.
examples.say: I am shaping my energy with my hands.

examples.html: I move slowly, like Tai Chi, to feel calm.
examples.say: I move slowly, like Tai Chi, to feel calm.

examples.html: I am responsible for my peace.
examples.say: I am responsible for my peace.

examples.html: I will meditate, because it is my medicine.
examples.say: I will meditate, because it is my medicine.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · “Good,” “better,” and richer words
grammar.html: Instead of always saying good, choose a stronger word. Excellent = super good. Wonderful = full of wonder and joy. Use them after the verb felt or was: My mind felt excellent. · My energy was wonderful. Rich words help you share exactly how you feel.

paras: Imagine your mind is like a glass of muddy water （混濁的水）. When you shake it, you cannot see through it — that is a busy, worried mind. But if you hold it still, or move it very slowly, the mud settles to the bottom and the water becomes clear again.

This week we practice moving meditation. Like gentle Tai Chi （太極）, we put a hand on the heart, a hand on the mind (the head), and we draw slow circles around our body to feel the energy. We are not just sitting — we are shaping our energy with our hands, and shaping a little more peace in the room.

Best of all, this medicine belongs to you. No one else can make your heart calm — that is your own responsibility. When you choose to breathe, to move slowly, and to say “I am responsible for my peace,” you become your own gentle doctor.

items: Why is meditation like medicine? 為什麼禪修像藥？
How can you “move” your energy like Tai Chi? 你怎麼像太極一樣「移動」能量？
Who is responsible for your peace? 誰要為你的平靜負責？

principle.lede: When your body is sick, you take medicine. Teacher Dom reminds us that when the mind feels “foggy” or “crazy,” we have our own medicine too — meditation. And meditation does not have to be still: we can move our energy.
principle.h4: You shape your own energy 你形塑自己的能量
principle.html: In Buddhism this is called self-reliance (自依止): peace and awakening are cultivated by your own practice, not handed to you by someone else. Your inner state is not trapped inside you — it radiates outward and changes the room around you. So when you shape calm, positive energy with slow, mindful movement, you give a gift of peace to everyone near you.

practice.note: Watch the “Meditation Monkey” (or a kids’ Tai Chi) video at least 2 times this week and follow the movements. Then choose the tier that fits you. 看影片至少兩次，跟著動作移動你的能量，再選一個適合的等級。

tiers.req: Draw yourself doing a Tai Chi move and write the word ENERGY in big letters. Then write 2 simple sentences.
tiers.ex: ExampleMy heart is wonderful. I love peace.

tiers.req: Keep a meditation log — record how many times you meditated. Write a short reflection using the words medicine and calm.
tiers.ex: ExampleMeditation is like medicine for my brain. It makes me feel excellent and calm.

tiers.req: Write a 6–10 sentence paragraph: “I am responsible for my peace.” What does it mean to you? How can moving your energy help when you feel stressed or angry?
tiers.ex: ExampleI am responsible for my peace, because no one else can calm my mind for me. When I feel angry, I move slowly like Tai Chi and my energy feels wonderful again.

quiz.text: Which word means “優秀的 / super good”?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Excellent means super good (優秀的). It is a stronger word than “good.”

quiz.text: Complete the sentence: “My mind ___ excellent after I meditated.”
quiz.expl: Correct: B. We talk about the past with felt: “My mind felt excellent.”

quiz.text: In this week’s lesson, meditation is compared to…
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Meditation is like medicine (藥) — you use it to heal your mind, just as you take medicine for your body.

quiz.text: Which sentence describes moving meditation correctly?
options: I am shaping my energy with my hands.
I am shaping my hands with my energy.
My energy is shaping me with hands.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. The pattern is “I am shaping my energy” — we move our hands slowly to feel and shape the energy.

quiz.text: In moving meditation, where do we put a hand to feel the mind ?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Heart → hand on the chest; Mind → hand on the head; Energy → slow circles around the body.

quiz.text: The slow, gentle movement we practice is like which exercise?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Tai Chi (太極) is slow and gentle — perfect for moving meditation and shaping calm energy.

quiz.text: “I am responsible for my peace” means…
options: my teacher must keep me calm
I can choose to make my own mind calm
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Self-reliance (自依止): your calm is your own responsibility — you cultivate it through your own practice.

quiz.text: Which word means “能量 / the feeling of power around you”?
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Energy = 能量. We move our hands to feel and shape this energy.

quiz.text: Choose the richer way to describe a great feeling.
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Wonderful is a stronger, richer word than “good,” “okay,” or “fine.”

quiz.text: When your mind feels “foggy” or “crazy,” what does Teacher Dom suggest you do?
options: wait for it to fix itself
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Just as you take medicine for a sick body, you can use meditation for a foggy mind — you have the power to shape your own energy.

title_zh: 在和諧中律動 · The Body’s Grace 身體的優雅

pills.label: Principle
pills.text: Moving Meditation · Body as Medicine 身體即良藥

objectives.html: Learn the words harmony and unique, and correct the spelling of wonderful and excellent. Describe inner states with “I feel harmony in my heart” and “Everyone is unique.”
Understand harmony as body and mind in agreement, and deepen the idea of the body as medicine. Through Anattā (無我), honor that each being is unique, yet all can live together in peace.

examples.html: I feel harmony in my heart.
examples.say: I feel harmony in my heart.

examples.html: I feel calm when I move slowly.
examples.say: I feel calm when I move slowly.

examples.html: I move in harmony with the class.
examples.say: I move in harmony with the class.

examples.html: Everyone is unique.
examples.say: Everyone is unique.

examples.html: My painting is unique because I used my favorite colors.
examples.say: My painting is unique because I used my favorite colors.

examples.html: Our class sings in harmony together.
examples.say: Our class sings in harmony together.

examples.html: Unique people can live in harmony.
examples.say: Unique people can live in harmony.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Adjective vs. noun
grammar.html: Unique is an adjective — it describes a person or thing: Everyone is unique. Harmony is a noun — a thing we have or live in: We live in harmony. Watch your spelling, too: it is w-o-n-d-e-r-f-u-l and e-x-c-e-l-l-e-n-t.

paras: One day the teacher asked the students to sit as silent as a mountain. First they listened to their own breathing. Then the teacher began to speak softly — a guided meditation — and the children let the calm voice lead their minds back to the present moment.

Next, the teacher stood up and led a slow Tai Chi flow. Arms lifted like rising water; feet stepped like floating clouds. Every child moved differently, yet the whole class flowed together — this is harmony. The teacher smiled and said, “When your body and your breath agree, you have found harmony in motion.”

The teacher reminded them: each child is unique — no one else in the world moves, breathes, or thinks in exactly the same way. And still, all these unique bodies can move as one. That is the gift of the body as medicine: a healthy body and a calm breath become the home of a peaceful heart.

items: What does “harmony in motion” mean? 「律動中的和諧」是什麼意思？
How did the guided meditation help the students? 老師的引導冥想如何幫助學生？
Why is it good that everyone is unique? 每個人都獨一無二，為什麼是好事？

principle.h4: Body, Breath, and Group as One 身心一致
principle.html: Harmony is not everyone being the same. It is many unique people — each with their own佛性 (Buddha-nature) — choosing to move, breathe, and live together gently. When your body is well and your breath is calm, the mind rests easily. Move your body with kindness, and the harmony you feel inside will spread to your family and your school.

practice.note: Write your sentences this week, and practice 3 minutes of silent meditation at home — sit as still as a mountain. Choose the tier that fits you. 選一個適合自己的等級。

tiers.req: Draw a picture of yourself and write “I am unique.” Then trace and complete two sentences about harmony.
tiers.ex: ExampleI want harmony in my world. Everyone is unique.

tiers.req: Write wonderful, excellent, unique, harmony three times each. Then write 3 sentences about the guided meditation using the word silent.
tiers.ex: ExampleI sat silent and calm. The teacher’s voice was wonderful. I felt harmony in my heart.

tiers.req: Write a 6–10 sentence paragraph using all four words. Explain why unique people should live in harmony, using the Tai Chi class as your example.
tiers.ex: ExampleAlthough we are each unique, our class moved as one in the Tai Chi flow. That excellent, wonderful feeling was harmony in motion.

quiz.text: Which word means “和諧 / things working well together”?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Harmony means things working well together (和諧). Unique = one of a kind; silent = quiet.

quiz.text: Which word means “獨一無二 / one of a kind”?
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Unique (獨一無二的) describes something special and one of a kind, like you.

quiz.text: Choose the correct sentence.
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Unique is an adjective: “Everyone is unique.” We do not say “is harmony” about a person.

quiz.text: Complete the sentence: “We live in ___.”
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Harmony is a noun: “We live in harmony.” The other words are adjectives.

quiz.text: Which spelling is correct?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. It is spelled w-o-n-d-e-r-f-u-l — one “l” at the end.

quiz.text: Which spelling is correct?
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Excellent has two “l”s and ends in “-ent”: e-x-c-e-l-l-e-n-t.

quiz.text: In a guided meditation, the students…
options: go to sleep on the floor
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Guided (引導的) means the teacher’s voice leads the students’ minds back to the present moment.

quiz.text: “Harmony in motion” in the Tai Chi class means…
options: everyone moving exactly the same way
no one is allowed to move
moving as fast as you can
unique bodies flowing together as one
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Each child is unique, yet the whole class flows together — body, breath, and group acting as one.

quiz.text: Add a reason: “My painting is unique ___ I used my favorite colors.”
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Because introduces a reason: “…unique because I used my favorite colors.”

quiz.text: “The body as medicine” teaches us that…
options: we should never move our bodies
a healthy body and calm breath help a peaceful heart
harmony comes from being the same as everyone
quiz.expl: Correct: B. When the body is well and the breath is calm, the mind rests easily — a healthy body is the home of a peaceful heart.

title_zh: 動態的和諧：瑜伽與太極的結合 · Harmony in Motion

pills.label: Theme
pills.text: Physical Yoga & the Art of Yoking 瑜伽與「連結」之道

pills.label: English
pills.text: “I am yoking my body and mind.” · “I move in harmony.”

objectives.html: Learn the verb “to yoke” (to join together) and the practice vocabulary: physical, harmony, unique, balance. Use the present continuous (“I am yoking…”) for an action happening now, and fix common spelling traps: gratitude (not gratitade) and special (where “c” sounds like “sh”).

Understand that yoga means union (身心合一) — the joining of body and mind. Practice Right Effort through movement, and use the body as a tool of cultivation. Through mindful motion (Kāyagatāsati, 身至念) we steady the mind and meet each moment with balance.

examples.html: I am yoking my body and mind.
examples.say: I am yoking my body and mind.

examples.html: I am yoking my breath to my movement.
examples.say: I am yoking my breath to my movement.

examples.html: I move in harmony.
examples.say: I move in harmony.

examples.html: I move slowly, flowing like water.
examples.say: I move slowly, flowing like water.

examples.html: Inhale, stretch high.
examples.say: Inhale, stretch high.

examples.html: Exhale, stay balanced.
examples.say: Exhale, stay balanced.

examples.html: My yoga pose is unique and special.
examples.say: My yoga pose is unique and special.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Present continuous & tricky spelling
grammar.html: Use the present continuous — am / is / are + verb-ing — for an action happening right now: I am yoking my body and mind. · She is breathing slowly. Watch your spelling, too: it is gratitude (not gratitade) and special, where the “c” sounds like “sh.”

paras: Long ago, teachers in ancient India used one word to describe the deepest goal of practice: yoga （瑜伽）. Most people today think the word means stretching, but its real, original meaning is “to yoke” （連結、結合） — to join two things together so they pull as one.

A farmer yokes two oxen with a single wooden beam so they walk in step and plow a straight line. In the same way, we yoke three things together: our movement, our breath, and our awareness. When the body moves, the breath follows, and the mind watches gently — they become one harmony, not three separate things.

This is why we practice Tai Chi flow and yoga poses like Tree Pose. Flowing like water softens the tightness in the body; standing in balance steadies the mind. The body is not separate from practice — it is one of our most honest tools. When you move with focus and gratitude, you are doing real yoga: yoking your heart and mind into one calm, balanced whole.

items: What is the original meaning of the word “yoga”? 「瑜伽」這個字原本的意思是什麼？
Which three things do we yoke together? 我們把哪三樣東西連結在一起？
How can moving your body help your mind stay calm? 律動身體如何幫助心保持平靜？

principle.h4: Moving Meditation 動中禪
principle.html: Right Effort is not only sitting still. When we move the body with awareness — keeping the mind on each breath and each muscle (this is Kāyagatāsati, 身至念) — we dissolve tension and train concentration. Movement and stillness are not enemies. To yoke them is to find harmony in motion, and to carry that calm balance into every part of the day.

practice.note: This week your homework is active. Spend time every day practicing the Tai Chi or yoga movements we learned. Choose the tier that fits you — the daily movement is the practice. 選一個適合自己的等級，每天律動。

tiers.req: Hold your Tree Pose (balance) for 2 minutes every day. Then draw yourself doing your favorite yoga pose.
tiers.ex: Trace & complete“I am yoking my body and mind.” · “My yoga is special.”

tiers.req: Do a 15-minute daily movement session. Keep a short log, using unique and harmony at least once.
tiers.ex: ExampleDay 1: I practiced for 15 minutes. I felt calm. Day 2: My balance was unique today, and I moved in harmony.

tiers.req: Do a 30-minute daily session, then write a paragraph (6–10 sentences) on why yoga is called “yoking.” Use physical, harmony, and gratitude.
tiers.ex: PromptHow does moving your body help your mind stay calm? Describe your 30-minute physical practice with harmony and gratitude.

quiz.text: What is the original meaning of the verb “to yoke” ?
quiz.expl: Correct: B. To yoke (結合／連結) means to join two things so they move as one — which is the true meaning of “yoga.”

quiz.text: Complete the present-continuous sentence: “I ___ my body and mind.”
quiz.expl: Correct: D. For an action happening right now we use am / is / are + verb-ing → “I am yoking my body and mind.”

quiz.text: Which word means “和諧”?
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Harmony = 和諧. Balance = 平衡; physical = 身體的; unique = 獨特的.

quiz.text: Which sentence is spelled correctly?
options: I feel gratitade for my practice.
I feel gratatude for my practice.
I feel gratitude for my practice.
I feel gradditude for my practice.
quiz.expl: Correct: C. The correct spelling is gratitude — not gratitade. Watch the “i” and the “u.”

quiz.text: In the word special , the letter “c” sounds like…
quiz.expl: Correct: B. In special, the “c” makes a “sh” sound: SPE-shul.

quiz.text: Which three things do we “yoke” together in this lesson?
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Real yoga yokes movement, breath, and awareness into one harmony.

quiz.text: During Tai Chi flow, the teacher says we should move…
quiz.expl: Correct: A. Tai Chi flow is gentle and continuous — we move flowing like water to soften tightness in the body.

quiz.text: Choose the correct yoga instruction.
options: Inhale, stay balanced. Exhale, stretch high.
Inhale, sit down. Exhale, run fast.
Inhale, stretch high. Exhale, stay balanced.
Inhale, close the door. Exhale, eat lunch.
quiz.expl: Correct: C. We breathe in as we stretch up, and breathe out as we settle into balance: “Inhale, stretch high. Exhale, stay balanced.”

quiz.text: Why does “Right Effort” include moving the body?
options: because the mind does not matter
because we must always move quickly
because mindful movement steadies the mind too
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Practice is not only sitting still. Moving the body with awareness (Kāyagatāsati) releases tension and trains concentration.

quiz.text: What does the Teacher want us to keep doing this week?
options: keep yoking our heart and mind through daily movement
stop all practice until next class
avoid moving so our bodies can rest
quiz.expl: Correct: A. When you move your body with focus, you are practicing yoga — so keep yoking heart and mind every day.

title_zh: 悉達多太子的人生旅程 · From Palace to Peace 從皇宮到平安

objectives.html: Learn biography and purpose words: wealthy, journey, purpose, Dharma Wheel. Use the pattern “I find ___ interesting because ___” to share an opinion, and describe a story in the simple past tense.
Understand why Prince Siddhartha left a wealthy palace to seek a purpose, and see the Dharma Wheel as the map that guides the whole journey. Comfort and riches are not the same as lasting happiness.

examples.html: I find the prince’s journey interesting because he gave up everything.
examples.say: I find the prince's journey interesting because he gave up everything.

examples.html: I find the Dharma Wheel interesting because it is like a map.
examples.say: I find the Dharma Wheel interesting because it is like a map.

examples.html: The prince was very wealthy.
examples.say: The prince was very wealthy.

examples.html: He wanted to help all people end their suffering.
examples.say: He wanted to help all people end their suffering.

examples.html: My purpose is to learn and to be kind.
examples.say: My purpose is to learn and to be kind.

examples.html: The Dharma Wheel is his guide on the journey.
examples.say: The Dharma Wheel is his guide on the journey.

grammar.h4: Grammar Focus · Simple past for telling a story
grammar.html: When we tell a story that already happened, we use the simple past tense. Many verbs add -ed: He wanted to leave. · She walked far. Some verbs change shape: be → was (He was wealthy), leave → left, see → saw (He saw an old man).

paras: Long ago, a baby prince named Siddhartha （悉達多） was born into a royal family. His father, the king, wanted his son to be happy forever, so he built a beautiful palace filled with gardens, music, and every treasure. The prince grew up wealthy, and he never saw anything sad. But inside his heart, he felt that something important was missing.

One day the prince rode outside the palace walls. For the first time he saw four sights: an old man, a sick man, a person who had died, and a calm wandering monk. The first three showed him that everyone grows old, gets sick, and dies — this is the suffering （苦） the palace had hidden from him. But the peaceful monk gave him hope: there might be a way to find true happiness.

So the prince made a brave choice. He left his palace, his riches, and his comfortable life to begin a great journey. His purpose was not to become rich, but to find a way to help all people be free from suffering. After years of searching, he sat quietly under a tree and looked deeply into his own mind — and there he found enlightenment （覺悟）. From that day he was called the Buddha, “the Awakened One,” and he taught the world the path of the Dharma Wheel （法輪）, the map that guides us home.

items: Why did the king build such a beautiful palace? 國王為什麼蓋這麼美的皇宮？
What four sights did the prince see outside the walls? 太子在牆外看到哪四種景象？
What was the prince’s real purpose for leaving home? 太子離開家的真正目的是什麼？

principle.lede: The prince teaches us that where we are going matters more than how fast we run. A right direction — the Dharma Wheel — is worth more than all the gold in a palace.
principle.h4: Direction over riches 方向勝過財富
principle.html: Siddhartha had everything the world calls success, yet he was not at peace, because riches without a purpose are like an empty shell. When he chose to live for others — to help everyone end their suffering — his life finally had meaning. The Dharma Wheel is the map that keeps that journey on course. Even at school, your purpose can be simple and noble: to learn, to be kind, and to keep your inner peace.

practice.note: Think about Prince Siddhartha’s journey. Choose the tier that fits you, and share what you found most interesting. 想一想太子的旅程，選一個適合自己的等級分享。

tiers.req: Draw the wealthy prince in his palace. Then trace and complete two sentences, and copy journey and purpose three times each.
tiers.ex: ExampleThe Prince was very wealthy. The Dharma Wheel is his guide.

tiers.req: Write 3 sentences about the story. Use the pattern “I find ___ interesting because ___” and two vocabulary words. Then meditate for five minutes on your purpose at school.
tiers.ex: ExampleI find the four sights interesting because they changed the prince. He was wealthy but he wanted to help others.

tiers.req: Write a paragraph of 6–10 sentences. Explain why finding a purpose mattered more to Siddhartha than being wealthy, and how the Dharma Wheel acts as a map for his journey. Use all four vocabulary words.
tiers.ex: ExampleAlthough the prince was wealthy, he felt empty until he found his purpose. The Dharma Wheel became the map for his long journey toward helping others.

quiz.text: Which word means “富有的 / having a lot of money”?
quiz.expl: Correct: C. Wealthy means rich (富有的). Journey = 旅程; purpose = 目的.

quiz.text: What is a journey ?
options: a long trip or path you travel
quiz.expl: Correct: B. A journey (旅程) is a long trip — here, the prince’s path toward enlightenment.

quiz.text: Complete the opinion: “I ___ the story interesting because it is wise.”
quiz.expl: Correct: D. The pattern is “I find ___ interesting because ___.”

quiz.text: Tell the story in the past. Which sentence is correct?
options: The prince is being very wealthy.
quiz.expl: Correct: A. For one person in the past we use was: “The prince was wealthy.”

quiz.text: Why did Prince Siddhartha leave his palace?
options: He wanted more gold and treasure.
He was bored of the music.
He wanted to find a way to end suffering.
He wanted to become a king faster.
quiz.expl: Correct: C. His purpose was to help all people be free from suffering — not to gain riches.

quiz.text: In this lesson, the Dharma Wheel is like a…
quiz.expl: Correct: D. The Dharma Wheel (法輪) is the map that keeps the journey on the right path.

quiz.text: A person’s purpose is their…
quiz.expl: Correct: B. Purpose (目的／使命) is the meaning or mission behind what you do.

quiz.text: Outside the palace, the prince saw four sights. Which one gave him hope ?
quiz.expl: Correct: A. The peaceful monk showed there might be a path to true happiness, while the others revealed suffering.

quiz.text: When the prince finally awakened under the tree, he found…
quiz.expl: Correct: C. He found enlightenment (覺悟) and became the Buddha, “the Awakened One.”

quiz.text: What is the big lesson of the prince’s journey?
options: It is better to stay comfortable and never change.
A true purpose matters more than wealth.
quiz.expl: Correct: D. Direction over riches — a meaningful purpose, guided by the Dharma Wheel, is worth more than gold.
